review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
My aunt called again about the civil service exam. She does this every Chuseok season, like clockwork — finds some cousin's daughter who just passed and drops it into conversation as though I asked. I said the same thing I always say. I'm fine. The studio is fine. Rent is covered.

She doesn't believe me. I know because she paused the way she does when she's holding something back, and then she said Umma worries. Which is just her way of saying she worries, but putting it on my mother so it lands harder. I didn't take the bait. Told her I had to go check the kiln.

There was nothing in the kiln. I just sat in the studio and threw a slab that I didn't need. The wheel was cold and the clay was too wet because I hadn't wedged it properly. It didn't matter. I wasn't making anything. I was just moving my hands so I wouldn't think about the conversation. Normal Wednesday.

Gallery owner asked if I'd consider doing a collaboration with a streetwear brand. Custom glaze finishes on sneaker displays. Good money. I said I'd think about it but the whole walk home I kept hearing halmeoni's voice — that clay remembers what your hands were thinking when you shaped it. Felt cheap even considering it.

Woke up to a message from Ji-hye. She got into the residency in Arita. The one we both applied to, the one that would have meant three months studying under a fifth-generation potter and learning noborigama firing. She got it and I didn't, and she told me by text at 7am like it was news about the weather.

I know I should feel happy for her. She's my closest friend in the studio and she works harder than anyone I know. But all I can think about is how I spent two weeks on that application, reworking my portfolio statement, asking my old professor for a letter, calling my mother to say I might be in Japan for the spring so don't plan the family trip yet. All of that for a standard rejection email.

The worst part is I can't even be angry about it openly. Ji-hye keeps texting me details — the studio setup, the housing, the firing schedule — and I keep sending congratulations emojis and exclamation marks. My face hurts from performing enthusiasm on a screen. I closed the chat and went back to bed. It's not even noon.

Nudge: "What were you hoping would be different after Arita?"

Response: That I'd come back with something my family could point to. Studying under a master in Japan — that's something even my aunt would understand. It would have bought me time.

Went to the studio after moping around all morning. Didn't want to, but the pieces for the January show won't fire themselves. Started trimming the set of moon jars I threw last week. The proportions on the third one are actually close to what I've been chasing — that slight asymmetry halmeoni always said separates hand from machine.

Nobody cares about that distinction anymore, probably. The gallery visitors want to know the price and whether it fits on a shelf. But I held the jar up to the window light and the walls were even, the foot was clean, and the curve of the shoulder did exactly what I meant it to do. That matters to me even if it doesn't matter to anyone else.

Nudge: "Who taught you to look at the shoulder curve like that?"

Still at the studio. Eun-bi came by to pick up her tools and asked about the streetwear thing. I told her I'm not doing it and she looked at me like I'd said I don't like money. She said I should be practical, that these collaborations are how young artists build a name now. I told her I already have a name, it's just small.

She means well. But there's something about turning the craft into content that sits wrong with me. Halmeoni spent forty years in the same workshop selling to the same neighborhood. Nobody filmed her. Nobody asked her to put her glazes on handbags. She just made things that people used, and when she died the funeral was standing room only. That's the kind of career that makes sense to me.

End of the day. Checked my account and the last gallery payment was smaller than expected — they took a higher commission this quarter for "marketing costs." Didn't argue. Just transferred Umma's share and looked at what's left. The emergency cushion is thinner than I'd like going into winter.

Nudge: "How thin is thin?"

Response: Two months of rent and studio fees. Maybe two and a half if I skip materials. It's not nothing but it's not enough to stop the math running in my head at 2am.

Sunday morning, studio's empty, just me and the space heater humming. Loading the kiln for the bisque fire — the moon jars plus a few smaller bowls I made for the lunar new year gifts. Every year I make a set for the family, rice bowls with a celadon glaze my grandmother used to mix by feel. I have her ratios written down in a notebook but I still adjust by eye the way she showed me, adding a pinch more calcium when the humidity is high.

Fired up the kiln, set the cone, sat on the floor with tea. The studio smells like dust and wet earth and something burning off the shelves. Nothing urgent. Nothing broken. The jars are in there doing what they'll do and I can't control it from out here. So I'm just sitting. The heater clicks off and on. That's the whole update.

Back-to-school PD week and I'm already crawling out of my skin. Eight hours of slideshow presentations about "aligning to district benchmarks" and "fidelity to the pacing guide." Same slides as last year. I checked. They literally didn't update the copyright date.

I sat in the back row and started sketching out this idea I had for a unit where students write and perform original one-act plays instead of the standard five-paragraph essay unit. Pitched it to my department head during break. She said it sounded "fun but hard to assess against the rubric." Then she handed me the same binder of pre-made lesson plans they give every year. I wanted to throw it across the room.

The thing that gets me is I know I could light these kids up. Last spring when I threw out the textbook for a week and had them do a mock trial based on "To Kill a Mockingbird," the energy in that room was electric. Kids who never talked were arguing case law. But that was a one-off I snuck in. The system doesn't want that. The system wants the binder. I keep thinking about that wilderness guide job posting I saw last week. Flagstaff, Arizona. Six-month contract starting January. I have the tab still open.

First week with students done. I'm wiped. Taught the same intro lesson five periods in a row. By fifth period I was on autopilot, hearing my own voice like it was someone else's. Came home, microwaved leftovers, fell asleep on the couch at 7:30. Don't have much else to say about it.

Woke up at 11 PM still on the couch. Opened my phone and scrolled through photos from when I was guiding trips in the Rockies. Three years ago. Feels like a different person. Closed the app, stared at the ceiling. Didn't go back to sleep for a while. Something about this week got under my skin and I can't name it.

Nudge: "That different person — do you miss him?"

Response: Yeah. I do. He didn't have a retirement account or a lease or a faculty parking spot, but he woke up not knowing what the day would look like, and that felt right. Now I know exactly what every Tuesday looks like through June. I traded the unknown for the predictable, and most days I tell myself that was growing up. Tonight I'm not sure it was.

Had a genuinely good day, which is worth writing down because those have been rare lately. I threw out my fourth-period lesson plan halfway through class. The kids were dead-eyed reading the textbook excerpt, so I told them to close their books and we spent the rest of the period doing a walking debate around the room about whether Gatsby deserved sympathy. They had to physically move to a corner based on their position and defend it out loud.

It was messy and loud and two kids got into a real argument that I had to mediate, but every single person in that room was thinking. That's the version of this job I signed up for. Not the binder. Not the pacing guide. The moment when something catches fire and you're just there holding space for it.

Afterwards one of my quieter students, Kai, stayed behind and said it was the first time he didn't watch the clock all period. That landed. I drove home with the windows down and music loud and felt something I haven't felt at work in months — like I was in the right place. I know tomorrow I'll be back to the binder for the observation cycle. But today reminded me there's a version of this that works for me. I just have to keep finding cracks to push through.

Nudge: "What keeps you from teaching like that every day?"

Response: Observations, rubrics, pacing calendars, standardized test prep schedules, a department head who measures success by data walls. The infrastructure isn't built for what happened today. I'd have to fight for it constantly, and honestly I don't know if I have the stamina to fight a system every single day just to do my job the way I think it should be done. Some days the path of least resistance is just opening the binder.

Labor Day weekend and everyone went camping up near Hood River. I almost didn't go — had a stack of essays to grade — but forced myself. It was fine. Good, even. River was cold and perfect, did some bouldering, someone brought a guitar.

But sitting around the fire Saturday night I realized half the group was talking about their plans: houses they're buying, weddings they're planning, promotions. And I'm sitting there thinking about a six-month wilderness guide contract in Arizona and whether I could break my lease. I didn't say it out loud. I just laughed along and poked the fire.

I don't know when my friends became people with five-year plans. Or when I became the one who still doesn't have one. It's not that I think they're wrong. It's that the idea of mapping out the next five years makes me feel like I can't breathe.

Met with the vice principal today about leading the new "innovation lab" elective they want to pilot next semester. It would be a project-based class, student-directed, no textbook, no standardized test alignment. Basically everything I've been wanting. She said she thought of me because of the feedback from students about my off-script moments.

I should be thrilled. Part of me is. But the catch is it's one period out of five, and taking it on means extra planning hours and serving on the curriculum committee, which meets every other Wednesday through May. More meetings. More structure layered on top of the thing that's supposed to be the antidote to structure.

I told her I'd think about it over the weekend. Walking to my car I was already arguing with myself. One voice saying this is exactly the kind of opening I've been asking for, stop being impossible to satisfy. Another voice saying one elective doesn't change the other four periods of binder teaching, and now I'd be even more locked in with committee obligations.

Called my sister on the drive home. She said "Marcus, you'd complain about winning the lottery." She's not entirely wrong. But I also don't think wanting more than one period of real teaching out of five is unreasonable. I just don't know if the answer is to take the deal or to admit this whole career might not be the right container.

The landlord for the apartment we've been saving for called Ernesto and said he's raising the deposit by two thousand pesos. Just like that. We were so close. I had the whole timeline mapped out in my notebook and now the numbers don't work anymore. I keep telling myself we'll figure it out but my stomach won't stop tightening.

Nudge: "What part of the plan changing bothers you most?"

Response: It's not about one part, it's about the whole thing. We had a deal, basically. We shook hands on a number and now it's different. But I guess that's how landlords are. Ernesto says we just push the move-out date back a month. That's probably what we'll do.

Sofia napped for almost two hours this afternoon, which never happens. I used the time to reorganize the kitchen cabinets at my suegra's house. She didn't ask me to but they were driving me crazy. Everything is in labeled containers now. Ernesto's mom didn't say much about it. Made arroz con leche after dinner. Normal day otherwise.

Nudge: "How did it feel reorganizing the kitchen in a house that isn't yours?"

Response: Honestly, a little strange. I know it's her kitchen. She's been cooking in it for thirty years and I show up and start putting labels on things. But when things are out of order I can't think straight. It's like the mess creeps into my head. I think part of me is trying to make this place feel like mine because we're going to be here longer than I planned. Putting the lentils in a jar with a label is the one thing I can actually control right now.

Ernesto brought up the motorcycle idea again at breakfast. His coworker is selling one cheap because he's moving to Monterrey. It would save Ernesto bus fare, which is like eight hundred pesos a month, and it would cut his commute in half. He showed me the numbers and on paper it makes sense. I get that.

But we'd have to pull from the apartment savings to buy it. Not all of it, but enough that the timeline changes again. And motorcycles break down. Insurance costs money. What if he gets into an accident? His factory doesn't have great coverage for that kind of thing. I didn't say all of this out loud because I could tell he was excited and I didn't want to be the person who always says no.

I said I'd think about it. Which is what I say when I already know my answer but don't want to fight. My mom used to do the same thing with my dad. She'd say "vamos a ver" and then quietly make sure things stayed the way they were.

I looked at our savings notebook after he left for work. If we buy the motorcycle we'd need four more months to save back up for the deposit. Four months is a long time when you're sleeping in someone else's house and your daughter doesn't have her own room.

Made Sofia's nine-month checkup appointment for next week. Packed her bag the night before like I always do—extra clothes, snacks, her vaccination booklet in the zippered pocket. Ernesto laughed and said I prepare like we're going on a trip. Maybe. But last time we forgot the booklet and had to come back. That's not happening again. Felt good having it all ready.

Nudge: "What goes through your mind when everything's packed and ready?"

Response: It's like I can breathe for a second. When the bag is packed and the appointment is confirmed and I know exactly what time we need to leave, there's this little window where nothing can go wrong. Or at least, nothing that I could have prevented. That matters to me. My mom raised four of us and she was always scrambling, always forgetting things, always stressed. I don't want that for Sofia. I want her to feel like someone has it handled.

The water heater has been making a weird sound for about a week. My suegra says it's always done that but I know it hasn't because I would have noticed. I told Ernesto we should call someone to look at it. He said it's probably fine and that getting a repair person out on a weekend costs double.

So we're just going to wait. And I'm the one who bathes Sofia in that bathroom every evening, listening to the thing rattle, wondering if it's going to give out or worse. He's at work all day, he doesn't hear it.

I know I could call someone myself but it's not my house. It's his parents' house and their water heater. So I wait for Ernesto to decide it matters enough, and in the meantime I boil water on the stove just in case, which his mom thinks is ridiculous.

Nudge: "You mentioned boiling water just in case — what would need to happen for you to call someone yourself?"

Sofia is teething again and didn't sleep more than two hours at a stretch last night. I lost count of how many times I got up. Ernesto tried to help but she only wanted me, crying harder when he picked her up, so he went back to bed because he had an early shift.

My suegra made us chilaquiles this morning and I almost fell asleep at the table. She took Sofia for a walk around the block so I could rest. I lay down but couldn't actually sleep. Just stared at the ceiling thinking about nothing specific.

Did laundry, folded everything, put it away. Made a grocery list for the week. The routine helps on days like this. When I'm too tired to think, my hands know what to do. Ernesto texted asking if I needed anything from the tienda on his way home. I said no. We have everything. I checked twice.

Turned down the director thing. Everyone at the office looked at me like I'd lost my mind, including Tomás who's been pushing me toward it for two years. I don't owe anyone an explanation for wanting to keep my Fridays.

Nudge: "What was it about the way they looked at you?"

Can't stop replaying dinner. Camila brought up the director thing again, not even an hour after I told her I said no. She had that voice, the one where she's being very calm and very patient like she's talking to one of her students instead of her husband. She asked what my "plan" was. I said the plan is the same plan it's always been, I do good work, I enjoy my life, and I don't let some title turn me into someone who eats sad lunches at a desk in a city I don't even like.

She didn't say anything for a while. Then she said maybe the problem isn't the job, maybe the problem is that I always need something new and nothing is ever enough. That sat weird. Because I don't think wanting to feel alive is the same as nothing being enough. But I also couldn't explain why she's wrong without sounding like I'm the one who doesn't get it.

Nudge: "Do you think she has a point?"

Response: Maybe a small one. I don't get bored with her. But I get bored with everything around us if it stays the same too long, and I think she takes that personally even when it's not about her.

Spent the whole day at the distribution center running damage control on a shipment mix-up with the Monterrey warehouse. Five hours of calls and spreadsheets and a very annoyed client who kept asking to speak to "someone higher up." Normally this kind of chaos makes me want to throw my phone off the roof, but today I sort of got into it. There was a rhythm to it. I called in a favor from Beto at the trucking company, rerouted three pallets through Aguascalientes, and had the corrected order confirmed by four o'clock. The client's tone shifted completely once he realized I'd actually fixed it same-day.

Drove home with the windows down, cumbia loud, and stopped at the fish market in Chapala on the way back. Picked up red snapper and a bag of limes. Made a zarandeado on the grill while the sun went down. Camila came out and sat with me while I cooked and we didn't talk about the director thing or plans or any of it. She just ate and said it was the best fish I'd made all year. That was the whole evening and it was perfect.

I think the good days look like this. Something that gets my blood going during the hours I'm working, and then the reward after. The snapper, the lime, the smoke off the grill, my wife telling me I got the char exactly right. I don't need a bigger title for that. I just need the space to keep having days like this one.

Budget meeting ran long. Signed up for a weekend mezcal tasting in Oaxaca next month. Tomás asked if I was "collecting hobbies" and I told him I was collecting experiences, which shut him up.

Nudge: "The mezcal tasting -- whose idea was that?"

Response: Saw it online somewhere. Looked fun, just booked it.

Sunday and I'm wiped. Camila's parents came down from Zacatecas for the weekend and I spent two days doing the good son-in-law routine. Don Aurelio wanted to sit in the living room and watch fútbol all afternoon and I sat there with him because that's what you do. Four hours of commentary about the ref and the state of Mexican soccer and me nodding along while my brain was climbing the walls. I kept thinking about the Sayulita forecast, perfect swell all weekend and I'm on the couch watching a replay of a game I already know the score of.

Camila noticed I was restless. She told me to relax and be present. I wanted to say that sitting still and watching something I've already seen is the opposite of present for me, but I didn't. I just went to the kitchen and made everyone coffee and let it go. By tonight I felt like I'd been holding my breath for 48 hours and only just exhaled.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd actually said that to her?"

Response: She would have said I was being selfish in front of her parents. And honestly, maybe she'd be right. They drove five hours to visit. I just can't fake enjoying something I'm not enjoying, and pretending drains me more than anything else.

Took the day off Friday and drove out to Sayulita alone. The waves were clean, offshore wind, barely anyone out. I caught a left that held up for maybe six seconds and when I came off the board I was laughing out loud with nobody around to hear it. Ate ceviche at that stand near the plaza where the guy recognizes me now and gives me extra tostadas.

On the drive home I called my brother Ernesto in Puebla. He's been grinding sixty-hour weeks at his plant and sounded terrible. I told him to come visit, we'd go out on the boat, eat well, do nothing useful for three days. He said he couldn't, too many deadlines. He sounded exactly like the version of me I've spent five years trying not to become. But surfing alone on a Friday while he sat in a fluorescent office tells me I'm doing the right thing, even when Camila and Tomás think I'm wasting my potential. I'm not wasting anything. I'm spending it.

The Hamburg project is on hold. The city council voted to delay the commission review until March, citing budget reallocation concerns. I found out through my gallery contact, not from the committee chair who promised to keep me informed directly. That's two months of planning, three revised proposals, and a scale model my team spent five weeks fabricating.

I called Martina, the head of the cultural affairs department, and she said the delay was procedural. No guarantees about the final decision. When I asked about the competing submissions, she went quiet. There are competing submissions now. Nobody mentioned that before.

My assistant Werner suggested we could use the time to refine the proposal further. I told him the proposal doesn't need refining. What it needs is for people to honor their word. The original brief came to me because of my work in Frankfurt and Munich. Now there are suddenly other artists in the conversation.

Spent the evening alone in the studio. Didn't work on anything. Just sat with a glass of Burgundy and looked at the model. It's good work. The proportions are right, the way it catches light from the north side. Should have been straightforward.

Nudge: "Why alone tonight instead of calling someone?"

Response: Who would I call? Werner just tells me what I want to hear. My ex-wife would say I brought this on myself with my ego. My collector friends would offer sympathy but secretly wonder if I'm losing my touch. The wine was better company.

Meeting today with the Becker family about their estate commission. Third generation textile money, building a new country house in Baden-Württemberg. They want something for the entrance courtyard. Five meters minimum, abstract but with classical proportions. Budget is essentially open.

Herr Becker junior did most of the talking. Young man, maybe thirty-five, clearly trying to establish himself as the decision-maker now that his father has stepped back. He kept referencing installations he'd seen in Basel and New York. Wanted to know my thoughts on incorporating water features. I told him bronze and water require careful engineering, which adds six months minimum to the timeline.

His wife seemed more interested in the aesthetic conversation. She asked good questions about patina development over decades. I showed them photos of my Frankfurt piece from 2008 and how it's aged. She understood immediately what I was explaining about surface evolution.

Left the meeting feeling solid about the project. Private commissions like this are cleaner. One family, one vision, one contract. No committees, no budget votes, no competing proposals appearing from nowhere. The fee will be substantial. I'm already thinking about how to spend the spring - perhaps extend the Mallorca stay into April this year.

Nudge: "Which of them do you think will actually decide?"

Response: The wife. Herr Becker junior wants to prove he's running things, but she's the one with the eye. He'll defer to her judgment on anything visual. I've seen this dynamic before with these families. The money gives orders, but taste steers the ship. I'll make sure my next presentation speaks to what she responded to.
Christmas dinner at my sister's in Cologne. Too many relatives, too much noise. Drove back late and opened a 2015 Barolo I'd been saving.

Received the formal letter from Hamburg today. The commission has been opened to a juried competition. My original invitation as the presumptive artist is now simply one submission among what they expect will be twelve to fifteen proposals. They frame it as fairness and transparency. I frame it as cowardice.

I built relationships with that cultural department for eight years. Hosted their delegation when they visited my Munich installation. Introduced Martina to collectors who later funded her gallery renovation. This is how it works in this world - you invest in people, build mutual obligation, and when opportunities arise, those investments pay returns. Now some junior bureaucrat has decided that giving me what I earned looks like favoritism. I may withdraw entirely. Let them have their competition with their fifteen proposals from artists who haven't done a single public piece over ten meters.

Nudge: "What would withdrawing actually prove?"

Response: That I don't need to compete for something that should have been mine by right. That my reputation speaks without having to perform for a jury of people who probably couldn't name three working sculptors. Maybe nothing. Maybe I'm just angry and this is how it comes out. The Becker commission will pay better anyway.

Had dinner with Joachim, an old friend from my academy days. He never pursued public commissions - works entirely through private galleries and collectors. Smaller pieces, more experimental. He mentioned he spent November in Portugal, just making work in a rented studio with no deadlines, no clients, no obligations. Came back with a series he's genuinely excited about.

I found myself envying that. Not the smaller scale or the lesser income, but the freedom from always positioning. Every conversation I have lately is a negotiation. Who gets credit, who controls the timeline, who has final say. Even the Becker project - I'm already calculating how to manage the son's ego while cultivating the wife's trust. Joachim just makes things. When did I stop doing that?

Nudge: "Could you actually work that way now?"

Response: I don't know. The studio, the assistants, the Mallorca house - they all require the income that comes from the positioning. But it's not just about money. I'm not sure I'd know what to make if nobody was watching. Joachim's work comes from somewhere inside him. Mine comes from briefs and site specifications. Maybe that's fine. Maybe that's just who I became. The question bothers me more than I expected.

New Year's Eve at the Düsseldorf Kunstverein event. Good turnout, the usual faces. I wore my grey Zegna and the Patek I bought myself after the Munich commission. People came over to talk, asked about my projects, laughed at my stories about difficult clients. The museum director mentioned they're planning a retrospective series next year featuring regional artists. My name came up in their planning meeting.

Drove home after midnight feeling satisfied. This is what the work is for - not just the objects themselves but the standing they create. The ability to walk into a room and have people know who you are and what you've accomplished. Joachim wouldn't understand this. He'd find it exhausting or empty. But there's genuine satisfaction in being recognized. I've earned that standing through decades of difficult work. Tonight felt like confirmation.

Spent the day in the studio actually working. Not managing, not planning, not reviewing contracts. Put on old clothes and spent six hours with clay, building maquettes for the Becker piece. Three different approaches to the water feature question - one that incorporates it, one that acknowledges the site's water table through the form without literal water, one that ignores it entirely.

My hands remembered things my mind had forgotten. The resistance of material, the way a form wants to go one direction but you have to convince it to go another. Werner came in at some point to ask about scheduling and I didn't even look up, just told him we'd discuss it tomorrow. By evening my back hurt and my shoulders were stiff and I felt better than I have in weeks. Opened a good Riesling and sat looking at what I'd made.

Nudge: "What made today different from usual?"

Response: No audience. Nothing to prove to anyone. Just me and the clay and the problem of how to fill that courtyard space. The Becker meeting, the Hamburg situation, even the Kunstverein evening - there's always a performance involved. Today was just doing the thing itself. I'd forgotten how much I used to need that. Maybe still do.

Presentation to the Beckers today. Brought all three maquettes. Herr Becker junior immediately gravitated toward the most literal interpretation - the water feature, the dramatic scale, the impressive engineering. He started talking about how visitors would experience it, the Instagram moments, the way it would announce the family's arrival into a new era.

His wife was quiet for a long time. Then she pointed to the smallest maquette - the one where the form echoes the water table beneath without any actual water. She said it felt inevitable. Like it belonged to the land rather than being imposed on it. Her husband looked irritated for a moment, then asked what I thought.

I told them the truth: the water feature would be magnificent and expensive. The subtle piece would be better art. I could see Herr Becker calculating - the subtle piece was also a third of the budget. For a moment I wondered if I'd just talked myself out of substantial money.

In the end they went with the subtle piece. The wife's choice. I'm genuinely pleased, which surprises me. A year ago I would have steered them toward the bigger commission without hesitation. The income loss is real but manageable. And I'll actually enjoy making this one. That matters more than I thought it would.

Nudge: "What changed in the last year?"

Response: Maybe nothing changed. Maybe I just got old enough to notice what was already there. The Hamburg situation reminded me that all the positioning and relationship-building can evaporate overnight. The status game has diminishing returns after a certain point. I have enough. The studio, the houses, the reputation - it's all established. What I don't have enough of is days like that one in January, alone with clay and no audience. The Becker piece might give me more of those.

Spent most of the afternoon sourcing fabric swatches with Diya at this tiny warehouse in Camp. The guy running the place kept talking about minimum order quantities and I could see her doing math in her head, getting stressed. I just wanted to touch everything, feel the weight of it. Found this insane indigo block-print that would be perfect for the bomber jackets we've been sketching.

On the rickshaw back I was thinking about how three months ago I was sitting in a lecture hall pretending to care about supply chain management and now I'm actually doing it, badly, in real life. The irony isn't lost on me. Mum called while I was out but I let it ring. Didn't feel like hearing about Priya's wedding venue shortlist again.

Nudge: "What happens when you do pick up those calls?"

Response: Same script every time. How's the business, when am I coming home, did I hear Priya's news. I just run out of ways to sound enthusiastic.

Pop-up at Koregaon Park went well. Sold eleven pieces including two of the bomber prototypes. One girl asked if we had an Instagram — we do, but it's barely set up. Diya handled the money stuff while I talked to people. That part I'm good at. Strangers telling me they like something I made with my hands, that's a specific kind of high.

Afterwards we went to this new rooftop bar Adi told me about. Spent way too much on cocktails but the music was perfect and the city looked ridiculous from up there. Diya said it was our "revenue celebration." We made maybe eight thousand rupees today and spent half of it on drinks and that felt exactly right.

Meeting with a guy who runs a boutique in Viman Nagar. He wants to stock five designs on consignment. The terms aren't great — he takes forty percent — but Diya says the visibility is worth it. I kept looking around his shop thinking about whether our stuff would even fit next to all that boho tourist stuff.

Adi came over after. We ordered biryani and watched some terrible thriller on Netflix. He mentioned his lease is up in April and sort of hinted at us getting a place together. I changed the subject to the boutique deal. Not sure why. It's not like I haven't thought about it.

Nudge: "You've thought about it but changed the subject — what came up for you in that moment?"

Response: Felt like if I said yes it would become this whole plan, this whole thing with routines and splitting rent and groceries. I like how we are now where it's easy.

Late now, Adi left an hour ago. Finished stitching the last sample for the consignment order and my fingers are sore but the jacket looks exactly how I pictured it. Small win. Going to sleep actually satisfied for once.

Went home for lunch yesterday because Dad's birthday. Mum cornered me in the kitchen to ask if I've "thought about what comes next" if the brand doesn't work out. Priya was right there smiling with her ring and her Infosys lanyard still around her neck like some kind of prop. I know Mum means well but I'm so tired of being the cautionary tale at family gatherings.

I told her I don't have a backup plan because this is the plan. She got quiet in that way she does. Dad just ate his cake and said the jacket I brought him was nice, which honestly was enough. I don't need everyone to get it. The brand is growing, I'm paying my share of rent, I'm not asking anyone for money. I don't know what else there is to prove.

Nudge: "You said Dad's comment was enough — was it actually?"

Can't sleep, brain won't stop. Thinking about the consignment thing and whether it's a step up or just giving our margins away for the ego of seeing our label in a shop. Diya ran the numbers and we'd need to sell through at least seventy percent to break even after materials. That's tight. But then again if we don't get the stuff in front of new people it just sits in our flat.

Also still chewing on the Adi thing from the other night. It's not that I don't want to be with him, it's that the second you sign a lease with someone the whole shape of your days changes. You eat dinner at the same time, you watch the same shows, you wake up and there's no surprise about who's next to you. That sounds awful when I write it out but I don't mean it in a cruel way. I just like having space where I can decide at midnight to rework a pattern or go meet Diya for chai without explaining.

Maybe I'm overthinking this. He didn't bring it up again and maybe he won't. Part of me kind of wants him to, just so I know he actually means it instead of it being some lease-expiry convenience thing. I should probably eat something, I skipped dinner and my stomach is doing that acidic thing.

Diya wants to pause the brand for a month because she got offered a paid internship at some design studio in Bangalore. She says it's temporary and she'll be back but it felt like the floor shifted. Sat on the balcony after she told me just staring at traffic, not sure what I'm supposed to do with half a brand.

Nudge: "What part of this scares you most — the business or doing it alone?"

Skipped the drive to Maman's yesterday. Told her the baby had a fever, which was half-true — she was warm, maybe teething. I just couldn't face the motorway again. Sat on the sofa with all three of them watching something stupid on the tablet and felt wrong the whole afternoon.

Nudge: "What exactly felt wrong — skipping, or the excuse?"

Response: Both, but differently. The excuse was just cowardice. The skipping though — it's like I broke something. We've gone every Sunday since before the kids were born. Papa sets the table for all of us on Saturday night. When I called to cancel I could hear him putting the plates away in the background and it made me want to throw up. One missed week and I'm already thinking, what if it becomes easy to miss the next one?

Clémence from the school pickup asked me why I bother driving out to my parents' every weekend when "nobody does that anymore." She said it like I was performing something. I told her it's just what we do, and she laughed and said her kids barely see their grandparents twice a year and everyone's fine. Good for her.

I don't know why it got under my skin so much. Maybe because part of me knows she has a point — the kids are exhausted by the time we get home, Hugo has football on Sunday mornings now and we keep pulling him out of it. My husband doesn't complain but I can feel his patience wearing thin when I insist we go even when it's pouring rain or one of them is sick.

But what's the alternative? We stop going and then what? My parents eat alone. My sister already moved to Bordeaux and barely calls. If I stop showing up, that table shrinks to two. I'm not going to be the one who lets that happen, even if Clémence thinks I'm being ridiculous about it.

Nudge: "You're pulling Hugo out of football for this — does he get a say?"

Called Maman tonight because Léa said something at dinner that nearly broke me. She said "Mamie's potatoes taste like your potatoes." She's five. She doesn't know what she's saying. But she connected it — the gratin, the way I do it with the nutmeg and the bay leaf exactly how Grand-mère taught Maman, how Maman taught me. Four generations of women and a potato dish and I'm crying over the phone like an idiot.

Maman laughed at me, which helped. She said Grand-mère would have said I was being dramatic, which is true. That woman didn't cry about anything. She just cooked and fed people and that was how she said everything she needed to say. I think I understand that now in a way I didn't when I was younger. The recipe box isn't just recipes. It's her entire way of being in the world, passed down in measurements and instructions.

I made Léa help me stir the béchamel tonight. She stood on the stool and held the wooden spoon with both hands. It was a small thing but it felt exactly right. Like I was doing what I'm supposed to be doing.

Sunday lunch went well this week. Papa made his cassoulet — the one he only does in winter because he says the beans need cold weather, which makes no sense but nobody argues. Hugo actually ate two bowls, which surprised everyone since he usually picks at things. My brother-in-law came without Sandrine, said she had a work thing in Bordeaux, and Maman pursed her lips but said nothing.

After lunch the kids played in the garden while the adults sat around the table with coffee. Papa told the story about his father's vineyard again — the one about the frost of '56 that killed everything and how they replanted by hand. Hugo was half-listening from the doorway. He's heard it before but I saw him lean in when Papa described his grandfather carrying the new vines on his back up the hill. These are the details that disappear if nobody passes them on.

Drove home in the dark. The baby slept the whole way. Hugo asked if we could come again next week and I said of course, that's what we do. It felt good to say it and mean it without any of the doubt from earlier this month.

Had coffee with my friend Nathalie this morning while the baby napped. She's going back to work full-time in March — her youngest starts maternelle — and she asked if I'd thought about it. I said not really, which is a lie. I think about it constantly. Before the children I worked at the préfecture, nothing exciting, but I was good at it. I had colleagues. I wore proper shoes.

But there's a voice in my head that sounds a lot like Maman, and Grand-mère before her, that says this is the work that matters. Being here when they come home. Cooking real food, not reheating things. Knowing their teachers' names, volunteering at the school. My mother did all of that and I never once came home to an empty house. I want that for my children too. But I also notice I've started counting the hours until bedtime some days, and that scares me.

Nathalie made it sound so simple — "You can do both." But can you? My mother didn't. Her mother didn't. And those families held together. I look at Clémence's kids eating canteen food five days a week and I think no, that's not what I want. But then I wonder if that's conviction or just fear of doing something different from what I know.

Nudge: "What would actually change at home if you worked?"

Father Michel announced the parish is cancelling the Mardi Gras procession this year. Not enough volunteers, he said. My parents marched in it when they were children. I brought Hugo for the first time two years ago and he still talks about the paper lanterns.

I told Maman on the phone and she sighed and said "that's how it starts." She meant it starts with one thing cancelled, then another, then there's nothing left. I signed up to help organise it for next year, which means more time I don't have. But someone has to, or it just disappears.

Nudge: "You said you're already counting hours — where does this time come from?"

Response: From sleep, probably. Or from the one evening a week I watch something for myself after the kids are down. I know that's not sustainable. But if I don't volunteer, who does? The procession just dies. I'd rather be tired than explain to Hugo why the lanterns aren't happening anymore.

Got rejected from another group show today. Third one this month. I know my work is good, the feedback from the last curator even said my pieces were "visually compelling" but "lacked cohesion with the exhibition theme." I keep telling myself that I need to find the right fit, but sitting here surrounded by half-finished canvases and a pile of rejection emails, it's hard not to question everything.

The worst part is I was so bored with my last series that I started experimenting with metalwork halfway through. Now my portfolio looks like three different artists made it. Maybe that's the problem. Everyone keeps saying to develop a "signature style" but the thought of making the same kind of work for years makes me want to throw my brushes in the trash.

Nudge: "What would it mean for you to have a 'signature style'?"

Response: Honestly? It feels like a cage. Like agreeing to draw the same box over and over because galleries can sell it easier. But maybe I'm also scared that if I pick one thing, I'll find out I'm not actually great at it. Easier to be "promising" in five directions than mediocre in one.

Still thinking about yesterday. Couldn't sleep so I went back through my old sketchbooks from freshman year. Forgot how much I used to draw just for myself back then, weird little creatures and impossible architecture, no thought about whether it would sell or fit a gallery theme. Somewhere around junior year I started thinking about everything in terms of what it could become, what door it might open.

Worked my gallery shift today. It was dead, which meant I had hours to just sit there and stare at the current exhibition. These stark minimalist paintings that all look almost identical to me, just slight variations in shade and proportion. The artist is represented in galleries across the country. People pay five figures for those canvases. And I genuinely don't know if I'm jealous or horrified.

The owner came in toward closing and we got to talking. She said something that stuck with me: "The artists who last aren't the ones who burn brightest, they're the ones who find something they can sustain." I nodded like I understood but I'm not sure I do. What if I'm just not built for sustainability? What if I need the burn to keep going?

Applied to a residency in New Mexico. Three months in the desert with studio space and a stipend. Deadline was today. Spent all morning putting together the application materials. Portfolio, artist statement, project proposal.

For the proposal I wrote about wanting to explore the relationship between geological time and human impermanence using found desert materials. It sounds pretentious when I read it back but that's what they want to hear. The real reason I want to go is simpler: I'm bored here. Same coffee shop, same studio, same loop of galleries on First Thursday. I think I work better when everything around me is unfamiliar.

Started tallying up my applications this year. Fourteen residencies, eight group shows, three grants. Got into two group shows and one residency that I ended up turning down because it conflicted with a commission. That's a fifteen percent success rate if you're generous about the math. Industry standard is apparently around ten percent so I guess I'm doing okay. But okay isn't what I'm aiming for.

Nudge: "You turned down a residency for a commission?"

Commission is done but I hate it. The client wanted "bold and colorful" and I gave them exactly that and now it's sitting in their corporate lobby looking like a very expensive screensaver. Got paid though.

Nudge: "What made you take this commission in the first place?"

Spent ten hours in the studio today working on a new piece. Lost track of time completely. My back hurts and I forgot to eat lunch but there's something on the canvas now that wasn't there this morning and that's enough.

Got the email this afternoon. I'm in. New Mexico residency, three months starting in March. I read the acceptance twice and then I just sat there staring at my screen for a while. Not jumping up and down, not immediately texting everyone I know. Just this weird quiet feeling, like something clicking into place.

Called my mom to tell her. She's worried about me being alone in the desert, which is funny because I'm alone in my studio most of the time anyway. But she's excited too. She asked if I was going to use this to "focus on one style finally" and I managed not to sigh into the phone. I told her I'm going to use it to make the best work I've ever made, whatever form that takes.

Started planning what to bring. Limited luggage means I have to choose which materials matter most. I'm thinking oil paints, my good brushes, sketchbooks, and nothing else. Let the desert fill in the gaps. Maybe that's what I've been missing, not a signature style but just enough constraint to push against. Every time I've made something I was proud of, it was because I had a problem to solve, not unlimited options. Something to think about.

My success rate for applications this year ended at nineteen percent. Still not where I want to be. But for once the math feels less important than what's actually happening. I'm going to make art in the desert. That's real.

Nudge: "You said constraint helps you make better work. How does that connect to how you feel about having a signature style?"

Response: Maybe they're not as different as I thought. A signature style doesn't have to mean repetition, it could be a set of constraints I choose for myself rather than ones galleries or clients impose. The desert residency is forcing me to pick only essential materials, and I'm actually looking forward to that limitation. If I could do the same thing with my artistic approach, choose my own boundaries instead of resisting all boundaries, maybe that's the middle ground. Still figuring it out.

My hands are still shaking from the critique session. Professor Mansour put my naskh panel up on the wall next to everyone else's work and asked the class, "Where is the artist in this?" Like I was just copying. Like three months of grinding ink and cutting reeds and adjusting every single letter weight was nothing. Somebody said it looked like something you'd find in a mosque gift shop. People laughed.

I didn't say anything. I sat there and took it. Walked straight to the bathroom after and just stood there gripping the sink. The worst part is I keep replaying it and I still can't figure out what I should have said. I know they don't get it. But that doesn't make the laughing stop echoing in my head.

Nudge: "What would you have said if you could go back?"

Response: That the script isn't decoration. That every stroke has a genealogy — my jiddo's hand over mine, guiding the qalam for years. That I didn't choose this style from a catalogue. But I don't know if saying that would have changed anything. They'd probably still just see an old alphabet on paper.

Spent the morning helping Teta Hana sort through jiddo's old papers again, she wants to frame some of his practice sheets, the ones with his warmup strokes, hundreds of little alifs in neat rows fading where the ink ran dry. She held one up and just smiled and didn't say anything. Put it aside for me.

Then I go to studio and Layla is laser-cutting plexiglass and Rami's doing something with projectors and sand and I'm sitting there with my inkwell and bamboo and I keep thinking do I look like a museum exhibit to them, like am I doing something real or am I just holding onto something because letting go would feel like — I don't know. I keep not finishing that thought.

Nudge: "Feel like what?"

Response: Like forgetting him. Like those practice sheets stop meaning anything. I know that doesn't make logical sense, I could still remember jiddo without doing calligraphy. But it would feel like I walked away from the table where he taught me and never came back.

Professor Mansour pulled me aside after class and said she sees potential in my work but wants me to "break the script apart." She used words like deconstruct, fragment, subvert. She wants me to take the naskh letters and tear them up, layer them over industrial textures, make them unrecognizable. She said it would be a strong thesis direction.

I told her I'd think about it. Walked home the long way through Gemmayze. Something about the idea makes my stomach tight. Not because it's technically hard. I could do it. I know how to cut letterforms apart and reassemble them. It's that I keep picturing jiddo watching me do it. And Teta seeing the result on a gallery wall and not being able to read a single word. I said I'd think about it and I will, but the thinking itself feels wrong.

Everyone keeps acting like I'm afraid of experimenting. I'm not afraid. I just don't think ripping something apart is the only way to prove you understand it.

Nudge: "What would proving it look like for you?"

Had dinner at Uncle Fadi's last night and my cousin Reem asked about my thesis. I said calligraphy and she nodded like that was obvious, like of course Nadia's doing that, and I wanted to snap at her. I don't know why. She wasn't being mean. She was being supportive. But the way she said "of course" made me feel like a fixed point everyone's already mapped. Like no one wonders what I'd do if I chose freely.

Then mama brought up jiddo and told the story about him lettering the sign for the pharmacy on Rue Hamra, how the owner cried when he saw it. Everyone smiled. I smiled too. It's a good story. I love that story.

But I sat there holding my plate thinking about Professor Mansour's suggestion and how if I told this table I was going to fragment the script and collage it over concrete textures, my grandmother would go quiet in that particular way she does when she's hurt but won't say so. And my mother would change the subject. And I'd sit there knowing I made a room full of people uncomfortable for the sake of a grade and a gallery slot. So I won't do it. But I keep circling back to the fact that I want to, or at least part of me does, and I can't tell if that part is ambition or betrayal.

Nudge: "You said part of you wants to — which part?"

Response: I don't know. Can we not do this right now.

I did something small today that I keep thinking about. I was in the studio early, nobody else there yet, and I pulled out a fresh sheet and started writing the bismillah in naskh the way jiddo taught me. Perfect form, clean lines. Then without really deciding to, I took a second sheet and wrote it again but let the letters drift apart, stretched the connections, let the lam curve wider than it should. It still read as the phrase. Just looser.

I stared at both sheets for a long time. The second one wasn't ugly. It wasn't disrespectful. It was the same words with more air in them. Teta could still read it. Jiddo would have recognized the hand.

I don't know what this means for my thesis. I rolled both sheets up and put them in my bag. I haven't shown anyone. Part of me thinks if I show Professor Mansour she'll want me to go further, push harder, and I'll end up somewhere I didn't agree to. And part of me thinks if I show Teta she'll look at the second sheet and ask why I'm being careless. So for now they're mine. Two versions of the same prayer, sitting in my bag, and I'm the only one who knows about both.

Can't stop thinking about today. There was this older man, Mr. Hernandez, and he reminded me so much of my tio — the way he called me "mija" and told me about his grandchildren back in Honduras. He needed his blood pressure medication but couldn't afford the copay, kept apologizing like he was asking for something unreasonable. And I just... I knew we have samples. I know where they are. I could have grabbed a month's supply and no one would have noticed or cared.

But I didn't. Followed protocol. Gave him the paperwork for financial assistance that takes six weeks to process. He thanked me anyway, squeezed my hand, said God would bless me for my kindness. Kindness. I filled out a form.

The rules exist for a reason, right? That's what my supervisor says. That's what I tell myself when I'm lying in bed at 3am staring at the ceiling instead of sleeping. The system works when everyone follows it. My abuela used to say you earn respect by doing things the right way, even when the right way is hard. She volunteered here for decades and never took a single bandaid home that wasn't accounted for.

But also — what is the system actually doing for Mr. Hernandez? He's 68, worked construction his whole life, and now he can't afford pills that cost the hospital pennies. I went into nursing because I wanted to help people. Real help. Not paperwork.

I keep telling myself I'll feel better about this tomorrow. I won't.

Nudge: "What would your abuela have done?"

Response: She would have followed the rules. She always did. But things were different then — there wasn't so much bureaucracy, people looked out for each other more. Anyway, it doesn't matter. I have to get ready for my shift.

Told my mom I can't come home for Christmas this year because of work. It's not entirely a lie — I could have requested off earlier. Just didn't.

Nudge: "Why didn't you?"

Response: Because part of me wanted an excuse. Everyone will be there — tias, primos, the whole family asking when I'm getting married, why I'm not going to church anymore, if I'm eating enough. I love them but I come back feeling like I'm failing at being who they expect me to be. At least at the hospital I know what I'm supposed to do.

Good shift today. We had a Guatemalan family come in — the grandmother only spoke K'iche' and was terrified. I don't speak it fluently but I know enough from my own abuela to say the important things: you're safe, we're going to help you, your family is right outside.

She grabbed my hand during the IV and prayed. I prayed with her, quietly, in Spanish. Felt right. The daughter cried when I came out to update them. Said they'd never been treated with such dignity at a hospital before. That's the job. That's why I'm here.

I hate the new scheduling software. It's supposed to be "fair" and "optimized" but it has me working Christmas Eve AND Christmas Day, back to back twelve hour shifts. Meanwhile Sarah who has no family obligations gets the whole week off because she requested it first. Cool. Great. Love that for her.

Called my mom to complain and she just said "Ay mija, that's how it is. At least you have a good job." Which I know is true. I know I'm lucky. I know people would kill for this position. But I'm allowed to be annoyed, right? I haven't had a Christmas off in three years. Three years of sending gifts instead of being there. Three years of video calls while everyone eats together.

Nudge: "What's the hardest part of not being there?"

Response: Watching them on the screen and knowing they're making memories without me. My little cousin is seven now and she barely knows who I am. Last year she asked my mom if I was the one who sends the presents. Not tia Valentina. Just "the one who sends presents." I'm becoming a stranger to my own family and I can't do anything about it because someone has to cover the holiday shifts and I'm the one without kids.

Worked sixteen hours. A man died on my floor — no family, no one to call. I held his hand at the end because no one should die alone. My family sent me a video of them opening presents and I couldn't watch it.

Nudge: "You could be there for him but not for them?"

Everyone's going out tonight. I'm eating leftover tamales my neighbor brought me and going to bed early. Told myself I'd call my mom at midnight her time but I'll probably be asleep. Another year of choosing this life over the one I was supposed to have.

Nudge: "What life were you supposed to have?"

Response: Married by now, probably. Living close to home, seeing my family every Sunday, raising kids who speak Spanish as their first language and know how to make tamales and go to mass. That's what my mom did, what her mom did. Instead I'm alone in an apartment eating someone else's cooking and pretending this is enough because I'm "making a difference." I don't know if I'm brave for building something new or just selfish for leaving everything behind.

Supervisor meeting today. She said my literature review needs "tightening" before she'll sign off on the methods chapter. What does that even mean? I asked for specifics and got a vague hand gesture toward the citation list. Spent the rest of the afternoon comparing my draft to the review sections of papers that made it into top journals. They're all cleaner somehow, more confident in what they leave out.

Ingrid asked how it went when I got home. I said fine. She's been picking up extra client work to cover the gap our stipend leaves, and I couldn't bring myself to tell her I might be another month behind schedule. She looked tired. I should have said something real.

Nudge: "Why the 'fine'?"

Response: Because admitting I'm struggling feels like admitting I made the wrong choice. We gave up a good apartment, a pension plan, weekends that didn't involve reading deadlines. If I'm not making visible progress, what was all that sacrifice for?

Revised twenty-three pages of the literature review. Supervisor meeting rescheduled to next week because she's at a conference in Berlin. Department coffee hour was cancelled again due to low attendance.

Grant application rejected. The feedback mentioned "insufficient preliminary data" and "unclear pathway to completion." I've been working on this for eighteen months. How much more preliminary can you get?

The younger students in my cohort seem unbothered by these setbacks. They shrug, submit elsewhere, go for beers. Maybe they haven't calculated their monthly burn rate. Maybe they're not watching their wife skip the organic vegetables at the supermarket because the regular ones are cheaper. I keep thinking about my old desk in Stockholm, the ergonomic chair the company provided, the predictable paychecks.

Nudge: "What makes the difference for them?"

Response: They can afford to fail. They're twenty-six, living in student housing, no one depending on them. I'm trying to build something at an age when most people have already built it.

Something small happened today that I keep thinking about. One of my students from the tutorial group, a first-year named Willem, stopped me after class to ask about the regression model I'd explained. He'd been confused during the lecture but didn't want to ask in front of everyone. I walked him through it again on the whiteboard, step by step, and watched his face shift from furrowed to open.

Teaching wasn't supposed to be the point. It's a requirement they attach to our funding, time carved out of research hours. But explaining something clearly enough that another person suddenly gets it, there's a satisfaction in that I hadn't expected. Reminded me of training junior analysts at my old firm, the ones who actually wanted to learn. I was good at that. People would request me for their onboarding.

Walked home the long way along the canal afterward. The light this time of year stays until past nine. For once I wasn't calculating anything. Just watched the boats and felt okay about being here.

Nudge: "What made today different from the grant rejection?"

Response: The grant was about me being judged. This was about someone else learning. I couldn't fail at helping Willem because there was no scoreboard. It was just useful or it wasn't.

Couldn't sleep last night. Ran the numbers again on our savings. If I don't secure additional funding by September, we're looking at difficult choices. Ingrid doesn't know I've started browsing job postings in finance again. Not seriously, just looking. Just in case.

The thought of going back after everything we invested in this feels like a specific kind of defeat. Not just professional, something more personal. Like proof that I can't handle uncertainty, that I need someone else's structure to function. My father worked the same job for thirty-four years. I used to think that was limiting. Now I wonder if he just understood something I didn't.

Nudge: "What did your father understand?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe that wanting more than one thing at once is a recipe for misery.

Department seminar today. One of the second-year students presented preliminary results that were, honestly, sloppy. Half the citations were missing page numbers, the statistical approach was questionable at best. But the professors smiled and nodded and said it was "promising early work."

When I presented six months ago, I got twenty minutes of pointed questions about my methodology. I spent three weeks revising based on their concerns. I'm not saying he didn't deserve encouragement, but the standards seem flexible in ways I don't understand. Maybe they see him as someone with time to improve. Maybe they see me as someone who should know better by now.

Same day, later. Ingrid found the job postings in my browser history. I expected anger but she just sat down on the bed and asked if I wanted to talk. Told her everything. The grant rejection, the supervisor feedback, the runway calculations, the feeling that I'm competing against people who have nothing to lose.

She listened and then said something I hadn't considered. She said we came here so I could find out if I could do this, and that finding out includes the possibility that I can't, and that wouldn't make the choice wrong. It would just make it complete. I don't know if I agree with her logic, but hearing someone say it out loud made the weight feel different. Lighter, somehow. Like the worst case scenario has been acknowledged and we're still sitting here, still together, still figuring it out.

Finished the authentication module for the logistics client ahead of schedule. Took a different approach than what they spec'd out — used a token-based flow instead of the session model they wanted. Should be fine once I walk them through it on Monday.

Nudge: "What made you change the approach?"

Response: Their session model had a scaling problem they had not noticed yet. I flagged it in a comment two weeks ago but nobody responded, so I just built what would actually work. If they push back I have the benchmark numbers ready.

Had a long call with my brother Faisal today. He has been at the same bank for twenty-two years now and just got promoted to department head. The whole family is celebrating. My mother called separately to tell me how proud she is of him, which — fine. He earned it. I am not bitter about that.

What got under my skin was when Faisal said, near the end of the call, something like "you should think about going back to a real company, you know, before it is too late." He did not mean it cruelly. He genuinely thinks he is helping. But the assumption underneath it — that what I do is somehow less real because I do not sit in an office with a title on my door — that has been following me around since I left the telecom job.

The thing is, I make roughly the same money he does. I pick my own projects. I took three weeks off last November without asking anyone for permission. I built a small open-source tool last year that people in four countries actually use. None of that registers as success in his framework, and honestly, it does not need to. I just wish the conversations did not always circle back to the same place.

Nudge: "What would it take for those conversations to stop bothering you?"

Response: Probably nothing they could do. I think the honest answer is that some part of me still measures myself by their yardstick even though I walked away from it. If I were fully settled about the path I chose, Faisal's comment would roll off. It mostly does. But "mostly" is not "completely" and that gap shows up at 11pm when I am lying in bed running the conversation back.

The logistics client wants me to come on-site three days a week starting next month. Three days. In an office, at a desk they pick, on their schedule. Nadia says I should consider it because the contract is good but I feel like my chest is tightening just reading the email.

Nudge: "What part of the on-site request bothers you most?"

Response: The loss of control over my own hours. Right now I start when I want, break when I want, and structure my day around when my brain is actually working. Sitting in their office from 9 to 5 means pretending to be productive during the two hours after lunch when I am basically useless, and then having to leave at five when I am finally hitting a good rhythm. It is just a stupid way to work.

Spent most of today going back and forth with the logistics client about the on-site requirement. Their project manager, a guy named Khaled, kept framing it as a "collaboration opportunity." I told him I have been collaborating fine over Zoom for six months. He said upper management wants to "see bodies in the building." That phrase — bodies in the building — is exactly why I left corporate work in the first place.

I offered a compromise: one day a week on-site, the rest remote. Khaled said he would take it up the chain but his tone suggested it was not going to fly. Part of me is already drafting a mental exit plan. I have two other clients and a lead on a new project through my old colleague Rami. The money would be tighter for a couple months but manageable.

Nadia thinks I am being rigid. She might be right. But there is a difference between being rigid and knowing what makes you actually function well. Every time I have forced myself into someone else's structure for long enough, the work gets worse, not better. I tried explaining that over dinner and she nodded but I could tell she was thinking about the school fees.

Khaled came back with their answer: two days on-site minimum, non-negotiable. I told him I would need until Sunday to decide. In the meantime, I reached out to Rami about the new project — it is a fintech startup building a payments API. Smaller scope, fully remote, three-month engagement.

Ran the numbers tonight. If I drop the logistics contract and pick up Rami's project, I lose about fifteen percent of my monthly income for the next quarter. After that it evens out if the fintech extends. Not comfortable but not catastrophic either.

Nudge: "How are you feeling about the decision itself?"

Response: Fine, honestly. The math either works or it does not. I should probably focus on getting Rami to confirm the rate before I commit to anything.

Cannot sleep. Keep thinking about whether I am making the smart move or the stubborn one. Nadia did not say anything tonight but she left the school fee spreadsheet open on the kitchen table, which said plenty.

Nudge: "Is Nadia's concern about money or something else?"

Response: Both, I think. The money is real — the kids' school is not cheap and we do not have a fat cushion right now. But underneath that, I think she worries that I will always choose the harder path when an easier one is right there. And she is probably not wrong. I just cannot make myself take the easier path when it means someone else deciding how I spend my days. That is not stubbornness, that is — I do not know what to call it. It is just how I am built.

Turned down the logistics contract this morning. Told Khaled it was a good run and I appreciate the work but the on-site requirement does not fit how I operate. He was professional about it. Rami confirmed the fintech project at a slightly lower rate than I hoped, but I negotiated a performance bonus clause that could make up the difference.

Nadia and I talked properly last night, not the tense kitchen-table version but actually sitting down after the kids went to bed. I told her I know the numbers are tight and I am not dismissing that. She said she was not asking me to take the on-site deal — she just wanted me to be honest about the trade-offs instead of pretending it was a clean choice. Fair point. It is not a clean choice. I gave something up to keep something I need. That is the deal and I can sit with it.

Picked up Lotte's kids from school again because she had a bad flare. Mats had a meltdown about his shoelaces in the car park and I spent ten minutes crouched on the tarmac retying them while Eva sat in the back seat reading like nothing was happening. That girl is ice. Bought them both stroopwafels from the bakker on Voorstraat and dropped them home. Lotte was on the sofa looking grey. I made her tea and put a load of washing on before she could say she'd do it herself.

Then I had to sprint across town for a delivery window I'd nearly forgotten about. Some law firm on the Maliebaan wanted documents by five and I made it with three minutes to spare, sweating through my jacket. The receptionist didn't look up. Forty minutes of cycling for eight euros fifty. That's the maths of it.

Got home, fed Poes, sat down, and realised I hadn't eaten since a cheese sandwich at eleven. My knee is doing that clicking thing again. I should stretch more. I should do a lot of things. The fern on the windowsill is browning at the tips, which probably means I've been overwatering. Even the plants aren't getting my best these days. Not complaining, just noticing how the day went.

Nudge: "What would complaining sound like?"

Sunday and I thought I'd have the morning to myself but Lotte rang at half seven because the downstairs tap was leaking and Pieter -- her ex -- isn't answering his phone as usual. Cycled over with a wrench and some thread tape. The washer was gone, ten-minute fix, but then she started crying at the kitchen table and I didn't know what to do so I just sat there. She said she's tired of asking me for things. That she feels like she's ruining my weekends. I told her that's stupid, she's not, but the word "stupid" came out too fast and she flinched.

I'm sitting on her front step now writing this. The kids are inside watching cartoons. I can hear Mats laughing. I want to go back in and say something better but I don't know what that would be. Sometimes being there isn't enough and you can't fix it with a wrench.

Later. Went back inside and Lotte had made coffee, which felt like her way of saying it was fine. We didn't talk about it. Played a board game with the kids -- Mats cheats spectacularly and Eva keeps a running tally of his crimes in a little notebook, which is both hilarious and slightly concerning. Normal afternoon. Good one, actually.

On the ride home I kept replaying the kitchen thing. She said "I feel like I'm ruining your weekends" and the truth is some weekends do feel like they disappear into errands for her. But I can't say that because it would confirm exactly what she's afraid of. So I carry it. It's not heavy most days. Today it's a bit heavy. Poes was waiting by the door when I got in, which helps more than a cat should.

Had a full delivery day for once. Eight runs between nine and four, mostly parcels for that organic food box service. My legs are cooked but the earnings are decent -- sixty-two euros before the platform cut. One of the customers, an older woman near Griftpark, invited me in for coffee. I said no because I was behind schedule, then felt guilty about it for the next two hours. She looked lonely. I don't know why that stuck.

My mate Jeroen called and asked if I wanted to come to his birthday drinks on Friday. I said yes but I'm already calculating whether Lotte will need me with the kids that evening. Haven't asked her yet. I should just go. Jeroen always shows up for me. I just need to show up back. That should be simple but somehow it never is.

Nudge: "What makes showing up for Jeroen feel complicated?"

Response: It's not complicated. I'll go. Just need to sort out the logistics first.

Lotte had a hospital appointment today. New neurologist, younger guy, very direct. He told her the progression is slow but real and that she should "plan her support network" for the next few years. She looked at me when he said that. Not at Pieter, who wasn't there. Not at our parents, who live in Friesland and visit twice a year. At me.

I sat in the corridor afterwards while she got dressed and I stared at a poster about hand hygiene and thought about what "the next few years" means. More school runs. More leaking taps. More weekends. That sounds terrible written down. I don't mean it that way. I mean I'm trying to understand the shape of what I'm signing up for, and whether it's something I'm choosing or something that's choosing me.

Rode home the long way through Amelisweerd. The trees are turning and the light through the leaves was that specific October gold that makes you slow down even if you don't want to. Stopped on the bridge over the Kromme Rijn for a few minutes. A heron was standing in the shallows looking patient. I don't feel patient. I feel like I'm holding something that keeps getting heavier and everyone assumes I'm fine because I keep showing up with the wrench.

Nudge: "You keep showing up. What would it look like to put the wrench down?"

Response: I don't know. Lotte has nobody else who shows up consistently. If I step back even a little, the gap isn't filled by someone else, it just stays empty. So putting it down doesn't feel like an option. It feels like leaving a hole.

Went to Jeroen's birthday. Stayed three hours. Drank too many Jupilers, talked rubbish, laughed until my stomach hurt. He gave me a hug when I left and said he's glad I came. Lotte had texted to say the kids were fine at their dad's for once. I cycled home through the quiet streets feeling lighter than I have all week. Small thing. Good thing.

Mum called from Leeuwarden. Wanted to "check in" which always means she has something to say. She asked how Lotte was doing and I gave her the update from the neurologist. Long pause. Then she said maybe I'm "doing too much" and that it's "not sustainable." She suggested Lotte look into professional home care. I told her that costs money Lotte doesn't have. She said maybe I should focus on my own life, find a partner, think about what I want.

Right. What I want. As if I haven't thought about that. As if I'm some martyr running around Utrecht with a spanner because I haven't considered the alternative. I do think about it. I think about it on every solo ride home. But thinking about what I want doesn't change what Lotte needs, and nobody else is stepping in, least of all Mum who calls once a fortnight from three hours away.

I know she means well. I do. But there's something in the way she says it -- "focus on your own life" -- as if helping Lotte is separate from my life. It IS my life. She's my sister. Her kids call me Oom Sansen because Mats couldn't say Sander when he was two and it stuck. I'm not doing this out of obligation. I'm doing it because I'd rather be tired than be the kind of person who steps away when things get hard. That's not something I need advice about from someone who sends a card on birthdays.

Nudge: "What kind of tired are we talking about?"

Response: The kind where you wake up already behind. Not sleepy tired. More like the tank is always half-empty and everything costs a bit more than it should. I manage. But some mornings I sit on the edge of the bed for a while before I stand up.

Got the renewal confirmation from Formosa Plastics this afternoon. Three-year contract extension, same volume, small price adjustment we can absorb. I sat with the letter for a while before telling anyone. Twenty-three years of this company and these moments still feel like exhaling after holding your breath. The terms are not exciting. No growth clause, no expanded scope. But when I walked through the warehouse floor afterward, I counted the stations — forty-two people whose paychecks run through what I decide. That number has not changed in four years and I am fine with that.

My son Jia-hao asked why I did not push for a bigger increase in the unit price. He pulled up competitor rates and showed me a spreadsheet. He is sharp, I will give him that. But he has not sat across from a procurement manager who controls a quarter of your annual revenue and watched them consider whether you are worth the trouble. You do not squeeze someone who keeps coming back. You make it easy for them to stay.

Mei-ling and I had tea after dinner and she said I looked lighter than I have in weeks. She is right. A locked-in contract means the next three years have a floor under them, and that matters more to me than a ceiling I might not reach.

Nudge: "What did Jia-hao say after that?"
