Second time writing today, which I almost never do. After the tea with Mei-ling I went back to my study to look over the Q3 projections and ended up just sitting there. Not anxious, not relieved — more like noticing the room. The desk lamp I have had since the company's first year. The filing cabinet that still has paper copies of our original business registration. Mei-ling keeps telling me to digitize everything but there is something about opening a drawer and seeing the actual stamps on the actual paper.

I called my younger brother Weijun in Kaohsiung. He asked about the Formosa deal and I told him it went through. He said congratulations and then started talking about his daughter's wedding plans. Normal conversation. But I realized I had called him specifically so someone outside the company would know the ground is solid. Like I needed a witness. Not sure what that is about. Maybe just habit — our father always reported good news to the family first, bad news never.

The neighborhood was quiet tonight. Mrs. Lin across the alley had her laundry out late again. I can see it from my study window. Small things like that — the rhythm of the block staying the same — I notice them more than I used to.

Had lunch with old colleague Lao Zhang today. We worked together at the engineering firm before I left to start the company. He retired last year from the same desk he sat at for thirty-one years. Full pension, government health coverage, the works. We ate at the beef noodle place on Zhongxiao East Road that has not changed its menu since we were in our thirties.

He told me he is bored. Gets up, reads the paper, watches his grandchildren on video calls, goes to bed. Said he envies me for still having something to wake up for. I told him I envy his pension. We both laughed but I think we both meant it. The difference is I chose the path where nothing is guaranteed month to month, and some part of me has never fully forgiven myself for that, even though the company has been stable for over a decade. I still check the accounts receivable every Monday morning before anyone else arrives.

I suppose that lunch reminded me why I keep the reserve fund where it is, even when Jia-hao argues we should reinvest. Lao Zhang's safety net was built into his employment. Mine I had to build by hand, brick by brick, and if I pull bricks out to build something taller, the foundation gets thinner.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to forgive that choice?"

Response: Honestly, I do not think I can. The choice was right — the company employs forty-two families. But the version of me that wanted a predictable life, that version does not care about being right. He just wanted to stop worrying. And I still have not given him that.

Jia-hao came to me with a proposal to take on a new product line — biodegradable packaging for the food service sector. He had the whole pitch: market research, supplier contacts, projected margins. Spent weeks on it apparently. And the numbers looked reasonable on paper. But "reasonable on paper" and "reasonable when you are the one signing the purchase orders" are different things entirely.

I told him we would revisit it after the Formosa contract settles in. He did not argue but I could see the frustration. He walked out of my office the way I used to walk out of my father's study — jaw tight, hands in pockets. I know what that walk means. But I also know what happens when you stretch into a new market before you have confirmed your existing revenue is truly locked down. I have seen it take companies apart. I am not going to let that happen to ours.

Nudge: "Did you tell him any of that?"

Mei-ling brought it up after dinner. She thinks I was too quick to shut Jia-hao down this morning. She did not say it that way — she said maybe I should look at the proposal more carefully before deciding. But I know what she means. She has been the translator between me and the children for decades.

She also mentioned that Jia-hao talked to her about possibly starting his own company if there is no room for his ideas here. She said it casually, while washing dishes. I do not think it was casual. I told her I would read the proposal again this weekend. Then I went to my study and closed the door.

Spent the weekend with Jia-hao's proposal spread across my desk. Read it three times. The margins are tighter than he presented — I found some cost assumptions that do not account for certification delays in biodegradable materials, which can take six to eight months in Taiwan. But the core idea is sound. The food service sector is moving that direction whether we participate or not.

I kept thinking about what Mei-ling said, about him starting his own thing. My father ran a hardware store in Tainan for forty years. Same location, same inventory categories, same suppliers. When I told him I was leaving the engineering firm to start my own company, he did not speak to me for two weeks. Not out of anger — he genuinely could not understand why anyone would walk away from a monthly salary. I swore I would never do that to my children. And here I am, not refusing to speak, but maybe doing something worse — keeping him inside a structure that is shaped entirely around how I see the world.

I circled three sections of his proposal where the numbers need work and left it on his desk with a note that said "Let's talk Monday." That is further than I wanted to go. But the alternative — watching him leave the way I left my father's orbit — I cannot do that either. I keep turning it over. I agreed to look, not to leap. But I am not sure the distinction matters to anyone but me.

Nudge: "Are you opening the door or just cracking it?"

Jia Hui came home with her spelling test today. 18 out of 20. The teacher wrote "Good effort!" at the top with a smiley face. I smiled and told her I was proud, put it on the fridge with a magnet.

But my mother-in-law saw it during dinner and said "Wah, only 18? Last time your husband always got full marks." I watched Jia Hui's face fall and I wanted to say something, anything, but I just changed the subject to the soup being too salty. I know she means well. I know comparison is how she shows she cares. Still scrubbing the wok now and can't stop thinking about it.

Registered Jia Hui for the enrichment center near the MRT. Three sessions a week, $800 a month. Told myself it's an investment, not an expense, but I keep opening the banking app to look at the balance.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to feel okay about that $800?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe if she comes home one day and gets full marks on something. Or maybe if I stopped needing her to prove that I made the right call leaving work. It's not really about the money, is it? It's about whether I'm doing enough. Whether staying home was worth it. Whether six years from now I'll look back and think I did right by her or I just threw away my career for nothing.

Ming keeps having nightmares. Third time this week I've been up at 2am holding him while he cries about "the shadow monster." Wei Hong sleeps through it all. I don't wake him anymore because he has that big presentation tomorrow, the one that might lead to the department head promotion.

I'm running on four hours of sleep and I still have to get both kids ready, meal prep for the week, and somehow fit in that phonics practice with Jia Hui because the enrichment teacher said she's "a bit behind the others." Behind. She's six. My hands are shaking from the coffee and I haven't even brushed my teeth yet.

Nudge: "What does "behind" mean to you right now?"

Response: It means I'm failing. It means other mothers are doing more flashcards, more reading, more something. It means by the time she sits for PSLE she'll already be playing catch-up. Everyone says don't worry, kids develop at their own pace, but then why does every other parent have their child in two or three different tuition classes? You can't have it both ways. Either it matters or it doesn't.

Parent-teacher conference today. Teacher said Jia Hui is "well-adjusted" and "a pleasure to have in class." Also said her math is strong but reading comprehension needs work. Suggested we read together more at home.

I nodded and wrote it all down in my notebook, the one I keep for tracking both kids' progress. Wei Hong couldn't come because of work, so I'll brief him tonight. Already ordered three new comprehension workbooks from Popular. Express delivery.

On the way home I saw one of the other mothers from the enrichment center. Her daughter is in Primary 2 already doing Primary 4 assessment books. She asked which tuition center we use for math and I lied and said we were still "exploring options." Didn't want to admit we only do the one enrichment class because I'm paranoid about the budget.

We have enough. I know we have enough. Wei Hong's bonus came in last month, we maxed out the CPF contributions, the emergency fund has eight months of expenses now. But I keep thinking about what happens if he loses his job, or if one of the kids needs medical treatment not covered by insurance, or if prices keep going up and suddenly eight months becomes four.

My mother used to say "save for nine winters" and I always thought it was superstition. Now I understand. You never know which winter will be the long one.

Nudge: "You said you have enough. Do you believe it?"

Response: Enough is relative. We have enough for now. But things change. I'd rather be overprepared than caught off guard. That's just practical, not paranoid. Can we talk about something else?

Christmas gathering at my sister's place yesterday. All the cousins comparing which schools their kids got into, which scholarships they're aiming for. Jia Hui sat in the corner playing with her cousin's old toys while the adults talked about tuition fees and percentile rankings. I should have pulled her into the conversation. I should have let her be a child.

Wei Hong asked if we really need the third workbook. Said Jia Hui looked tired this week. I told him I'm the one here every day, I see what she needs. He dropped it. Maybe I shouldn't have snapped.

Nudge: "What do you think she needs?"

Response: The same thing every kid needs. To be prepared. To not struggle later. He doesn't see how the other kids are doing, he doesn't sit in those parent WhatsApp groups reading about everyone's tutors and schedules. He gets to be the fun parent who comes home for dinner. I'm the one who has to make sure she's ready for the real world.

New year. Everyone posting their resolutions online. "More quality time with family." "Less screen time." "Be present." I used to make those lists too. Detailed ones, with deadlines and KPIs like I was still writing performance reviews.

This morning Jia Hui asked if we could just play today. No workbooks, no practice. Just play. And I said yes. We built a blanket fort and read picture books inside it while Ming napped. She laughed so hard at the silly voices I made for the characters. For two hours I forgot about enrichment schedules and comprehension scores and whether we're saving enough.

Then tonight, putting her to bed, she said "Mama, today was the best day." And I don't know why but I started crying after I closed her door. Is this what I've been missing? Is this what she needed all along? I keep score so carefully but I'm not sure I'm measuring the right things anymore.

Nudge: "What would measuring "the right things" look like?"

Response: Maybe it's not about measuring at all. Maybe that's the problem. I spent so many years in a job where everything had metrics, targets, quarterly reviews. I brought all of that home with me. Milestone charts. Developmental benchmarks. Comparison tables. But she's not a project. She's a person. A small person who just wants her mother to be there, actually there, not checking boxes in her head. I don't know how to stop keeping score. But today felt like the first time in months I wasn't playing a game where I'm always losing.

Turned down the director thing. Everyone at the office looked at me like I'd lost my mind, including Tomás who's been pushing me toward it for two years. I don't owe anyone an explanation for wanting to keep my Fridays.

Nudge: "What was it about the way they looked at you?"

Can't stop replaying dinner. Camila brought up the director thing again, not even an hour after I told her I said no. She had that voice, the one where she's being very calm and very patient like she's talking to one of her students instead of her husband. She asked what my "plan" was. I said the plan is the same plan it's always been, I do good work, I enjoy my life, and I don't let some title turn me into someone who eats sad lunches at a desk in a city I don't even like.

She didn't say anything for a while. Then she said maybe the problem isn't the job, maybe the problem is that I always need something new and nothing is ever enough. That sat weird. Because I don't think wanting to feel alive is the same as nothing being enough. But I also couldn't explain why she's wrong without sounding like I'm the one who doesn't get it.

Nudge: "Do you think she has a point?"

Response: Maybe a small one. I don't get bored with her. But I get bored with everything around us if it stays the same too long, and I think she takes that personally even when it's not about her.

Spent the whole day at the distribution center running damage control on a shipment mix-up with the Monterrey warehouse. Five hours of calls and spreadsheets and a very annoyed client who kept asking to speak to "someone higher up." Normally this kind of chaos makes me want to throw my phone off the roof, but today I sort of got into it. There was a rhythm to it. I called in a favor from Beto at the trucking company, rerouted three pallets through Aguascalientes, and had the corrected order confirmed by four o'clock. The client's tone shifted completely once he realized I'd actually fixed it same-day.

Drove home with the windows down, cumbia loud, and stopped at the fish market in Chapala on the way back. Picked up red snapper and a bag of limes. Made a zarandeado on the grill while the sun went down. Camila came out and sat with me while I cooked and we didn't talk about the director thing or plans or any of it. She just ate and said it was the best fish I'd made all year. That was the whole evening and it was perfect.

I think the good days look like this. Something that gets my blood going during the hours I'm working, and then the reward after. The snapper, the lime, the smoke off the grill, my wife telling me I got the char exactly right. I don't need a bigger title for that. I just need the space to keep having days like this one.

Budget meeting ran long. Signed up for a weekend mezcal tasting in Oaxaca next month. Tomás asked if I was "collecting hobbies" and I told him I was collecting experiences, which shut him up.

Nudge: "The mezcal tasting -- whose idea was that?"

Response: Saw it online somewhere. Looked fun, just booked it.

Sunday and I'm wiped. Camila's parents came down from Zacatecas for the weekend and I spent two days doing the good son-in-law routine. Don Aurelio wanted to sit in the living room and watch fútbol all afternoon and I sat there with him because that's what you do. Four hours of commentary about the ref and the state of Mexican soccer and me nodding along while my brain was climbing the walls. I kept thinking about the Sayulita forecast, perfect swell all weekend and I'm on the couch watching a replay of a game I already know the score of.

Camila noticed I was restless. She told me to relax and be present. I wanted to say that sitting still and watching something I've already seen is the opposite of present for me, but I didn't. I just went to the kitchen and made everyone coffee and let it go. By tonight I felt like I'd been holding my breath for 48 hours and only just exhaled.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd actually said that to her?"

Response: She would have said I was being selfish in front of her parents. And honestly, maybe she'd be right. They drove five hours to visit. I just can't fake enjoying something I'm not enjoying, and pretending drains me more than anything else.

Took the day off Friday and drove out to Sayulita alone. The waves were clean, offshore wind, barely anyone out. I caught a left that held up for maybe six seconds and when I came off the board I was laughing out loud with nobody around to hear it. Ate ceviche at that stand near the plaza where the guy recognizes me now and gives me extra tostadas.

On the drive home I called my brother Ernesto in Puebla. He's been grinding sixty-hour weeks at his plant and sounded terrible. I told him to come visit, we'd go out on the boat, eat well, do nothing useful for three days. He said he couldn't, too many deadlines. He sounded exactly like the version of me I've spent five years trying not to become. But surfing alone on a Friday while he sat in a fluorescent office tells me I'm doing the right thing, even when Camila and Tomás think I'm wasting my potential. I'm not wasting anything. I'm spending it.

Got home late from school and Leticia told me the water heater is making that clicking sound again. I went down to check and the pilot light was fine, but the tank had some rust along the bottom edge. Called Chema, our plumber, and he said he could come look at it Saturday. That is four days away.

I keep thinking about it. The thing is fourteen years old and I have been patching it along because replacing it is at least twelve thousand pesos we were not planning to spend this month. Sofia needs new uniform shoes and Valentina has her graduation fees coming up. I told Leticia we would figure it out but I could not stop checking the heater before bed, like staring at it would help anything. Too tired to think straight.

Nudge: "What's the worst part — the heater or the money?"

Cannot sleep. Leticia is out cold and I am sitting in the kitchen with the lights off because I did not want to wake anyone. I keep going over the numbers in my head. The aguinaldo comes in a couple of weeks but I already had that earmarked for Valentina's graduation expenses and the car insurance renewal in January. If the heater goes, I either pull from the emergency fund or I take on a Saturday tutoring group again, which I swore I would stop doing because it was eating into the only time I have with the girls.

What bothers me is that I should have replaced it two years ago when Chema first mentioned the corrosion. It would have been easier then. The repair fund had more in it and we did not have the graduation costs. I kept telling myself one more year, one more year. Now I am sitting here at midnight doing math on the back of a receipt.

Leticia would say I worry too much. Maybe. But somebody has to keep track of these things or they pile up until you cannot move. My father never had a plan for anything and I spent half my childhood watching my mother scramble. I will not put my family through that. I just wish planning ahead did not feel like carrying rocks uphill sometimes.

Nudge: "When you say carrying rocks — is this the same weight your mom carried?"

Response: Different rocks, same hill maybe. She was reacting to problems that already exploded. I am trying to stop them before they start. But the feeling of never being able to rest, of always watching for the next thing that could go wrong — yes, that part is probably the same. I never thought about it that way until just now.

Good day actually. Chema came early, turns out he had a cancellation, and the heater just needs a new thermocouple and some descaling. Eight hundred pesos. I could have kissed the man. Leticia made him a plate of pozole and he sat with us for a bit talking about his nephew who just started at the school.

Walked to the market with the girls after. Valentina tried on shoes at Don Beto's stall, the one where we always go because he gives the teachers a discount. Found a pair that fits her. Came home and sat on the patio while Leticia watered the plants. One of those afternoons where everything just fits into place and you do not have to push anything along.

The principal announced they are restructuring the math department next semester. Two positions may be consolidated. I have seniority but nothing official was said about who stays or goes.

Nudge: "What went through your head when you heard that?"

Response: Honestly my first thought was whether nineteen years counts for anything anymore. My second was checking how much we have in savings. Then I thought about Valentina's graduation in March and how I cannot afford any disruption right now. I did not say any of this out loud at the meeting. I just nodded and asked when we would get more details.

Leticia thinks I am overreacting about the department restructuring. She says my contract is solid and the union would never let them cut someone with my tenure. She is probably right. But she was not in that meeting. She did not see how the principal avoided eye contact with Profesora Marta, who has been there almost as long as I have and teaches the same subjects.

A colleague, Héctor, told me I should use this as leverage to finally apply to the Tec de Monterrey satellite campus. Better pay, better facilities. He has been saying this for years. I told him I did not want to start over at fifty-one, learning a new system, new administration, new commute. He laughed and said I was going to die at that desk. Maybe he is right too. But I would rather know what is coming than gamble on something looking better from the outside.

My mother called tonight and I did not tell her about any of it because she would worry. Told her the girls are doing well, which is true. Sofia got the highest mark in her English class. I held onto that for a few minutes after we hung up. Some things are solid. I just need the rest to stop shifting.

Nudge: "Héctor's idea bothers you — is it because it's bad advice or because part of you agrees?"

Response: Both, and that is the problem. I know the pay would be better and the school is newer. But I also know that private institutions restructure all the time and there is no union. At least here I have protections. The thing is, if I had applied five years ago when things were calmer, it would have been a measured decision. Now it would feel like running away from a fire, and decisions made while running rarely end well. So I stay and I wait for more information. That is not overreacting. That is just being careful.

The restructuring memo came out today. My position is safe. They are merging the two junior algebra sections and cutting one adjunct contract. I should feel relieved and I do, mostly. But I also feel something else I cannot name properly. Like I spent ten days bracing for impact and now that it did not come I do not know what to do with all the tension I was holding.

Héctor congratulated me and brought it up again about applying to the Tec. I told him I would think about it in the summer when things are quieter. He gave me that look like he knows I will not. And maybe he is right. But I also noticed I did not say no this time. I said later. I am not sure what the difference is yet but it felt like something shifted, even if just a little.

Had the regional partners call this morning and I could feel the whole thing slipping. The Vietnam office wants to renegotiate their revenue share, which fine, they can ask, but the way Minh framed it — presenting the new terms as a done deal in front of the whole team, slides already formatted — that's not a negotiation, that's a corner. He backed me into agreeing to "review" the proposal by Friday. Everyone on the call saw it.

Spent the rest of the morning replaying the moment. I should have cut him off. Should have said we'll discuss it offline. Instead I sat there nodding like I was considering it when really I was just blindsided. The Singapore guys were quiet, which means they noticed. Probably already talking about it in their group chat.

Called my CFO after to get the actual numbers. The Vietnam office is profitable but not indispensable. I need to figure out how to walk this back without looking like I'm retaliating. But honestly, part of me wants to fly down there next week and have the conversation face to face, in my building, at my table. Let Minh present his slides then.

Nudge: "What would've been different if Minh raised it privately first?"

Response: Everything. If he'd come to me one-on-one, I'd have heard him out, probably even agreed to some of it. The numbers aren't unreasonable. It's the staging of it that I can't let stand. You don't ambush the person who signs your contracts.
Booked the flight to Ho Chi Minh City for Tuesday. Told Minh I want to walk the warehouse floor and review Q3 ops in person. Didn't mention the revenue share. Let him wonder.

Back from Vietnam. The meeting went how I wanted — Minh pulled back to a five-percent adjustment instead of twelve, and I agreed on the spot so it looked generous. Shook hands in front of his team. I even took everyone to dinner after at that French place on Dong Khoi. Good evening, lots of laughing, Minh's deputy told me she's glad I came down personally.

But on the flight home something kept bothering me. Minh's face when I steered the conversation. He knew exactly what I was doing. He went along with it because he had to, not because he wanted to. I got what I needed, and the office stays profitable, and the relationship is technically intact. So why does it feel like I won a hand of poker against someone who couldn't afford to fold.

Nudge: "You said the relationship is 'technically intact' — what's the non-technical version?"

Response: Minh will do what I need him to do. But the next time he has a real problem — something going wrong on the ground — I'm probably the last person he calls. That's the non-technical version.

Alumni dinner last night went well. Three founders from the 2018 cohort asked me to mentor their series B pitch. Felt good to be the one they came to, specifically because they'd heard I don't sugarcoat.

Soyeon called from Zurich this morning, first time in two weeks. She sounded fine, talked about her roommate and some lake trip the school organized. Normal teenage stuff. But she didn't ask about home. Not about her mother, not about the dog, nothing. Just called to tell me she needed the tuition wire for the supplementary program, and could I do it today.

I did it immediately, obviously. Opened the banking app while we were still on the phone, confirmed the transfer, told her it was done. She said thanks, appa, and hung up. The whole call was maybe four minutes.

My wife would say this is exactly what she warned about — that sending Soyeon away was about my idea of what her life should look like, not hers. And maybe she's right that the school is more about the name and the network than what Soyeon actually needs at sixteen. But I also know what doors open when you have that name on your transcript. I know because I didn't have it and I spent ten years kicking down doors that would have just opened.

Sitting here now after the call, I keep thinking about the four minutes. She got what she needed and hung up. Efficient. I taught her that, I think.

Nudge: "Is 'efficient' how you want her to be with you?"

Response: No. But I don't know how to want something different without undoing the thing I built for her. The whole point of the school, the network, all of it — it's so she never has to struggle the way I did. If the cost is that she treats me like a service provider, maybe that's just what it costs. I just didn't think it would feel like this. Like I'm being handled by my own daughter.
Kept turning over that call with Soyeon. Ended up texting her a photo of the dog sleeping on her old blanket. She sent back a heart emoji. Stupid small thing but I stared at it for a while.

Long week closing the Series C extension. Twelve-hour days, four different law firms, and a lead investor who kept changing the valuation cap at the last minute. I held the line on the terms I wanted but it took everything. By Thursday I was running on instant coffee and sheer stubbornness.

Signed this afternoon. My cofounder — well, he's technically the CTO but he still calls himself cofounder even though he came on in year three — opened a bottle of something expensive in the conference room. Everyone clapped. I said a few words, mostly thanking the team, but honestly the thing I felt most was relief. Not celebration, just the weight coming off. Went home, ate leftover jjigae standing at the kitchen counter, and now I'm sitting on the couch and I can't even turn on the TV. Just tired in a way sleep probably won't fix.

Nudge: "What would celebrating actually look like for you?"

Response: I honestly don't know anymore. Used to be it was the next deal, the next target. Right now I think it would be someone saying, unprompted, that I did well. Not the team clapping because they're supposed to. Just one person who gets what it took.

Jia Hui came home with her spelling test today. 18 out of 20. The teacher wrote "Good effort!" at the top with a smiley face. I smiled and told her I was proud, put it on the fridge with a magnet.

But my mother-in-law saw it during dinner and said "Wah, only 18? Last time your husband always got full marks." I watched Jia Hui's face fall and I wanted to say something, anything, but I just changed the subject to the soup being too salty. I know she means well. I know comparison is how she shows she cares. Still scrubbing the wok now and can't stop thinking about it.

Registered Jia Hui for the enrichment center near the MRT. Three sessions a week, $800 a month. Told myself it's an investment, not an expense, but I keep opening the banking app to look at the balance.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to feel okay about that $800?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe if she comes home one day and gets full marks on something. Or maybe if I stopped needing her to prove that I made the right call leaving work. It's not really about the money, is it? It's about whether I'm doing enough. Whether staying home was worth it. Whether six years from now I'll look back and think I did right by her or I just threw away my career for nothing.

Ming keeps having nightmares. Third time this week I've been up at 2am holding him while he cries about "the shadow monster." Wei Hong sleeps through it all. I don't wake him anymore because he has that big presentation tomorrow, the one that might lead to the department head promotion.

I'm running on four hours of sleep and I still have to get both kids ready, meal prep for the week, and somehow fit in that phonics practice with Jia Hui because the enrichment teacher said she's "a bit behind the others." Behind. She's six. My hands are shaking from the coffee and I haven't even brushed my teeth yet.

Nudge: "What does "behind" mean to you right now?"

Response: It means I'm failing. It means other mothers are doing more flashcards, more reading, more something. It means by the time she sits for PSLE she'll already be playing catch-up. Everyone says don't worry, kids develop at their own pace, but then why does every other parent have their child in two or three different tuition classes? You can't have it both ways. Either it matters or it doesn't.

Parent-teacher conference today. Teacher said Jia Hui is "well-adjusted" and "a pleasure to have in class." Also said her math is strong but reading comprehension needs work. Suggested we read together more at home.

I nodded and wrote it all down in my notebook, the one I keep for tracking both kids' progress. Wei Hong couldn't come because of work, so I'll brief him tonight. Already ordered three new comprehension workbooks from Popular. Express delivery.

On the way home I saw one of the other mothers from the enrichment center. Her daughter is in Primary 2 already doing Primary 4 assessment books. She asked which tuition center we use for math and I lied and said we were still "exploring options." Didn't want to admit we only do the one enrichment class because I'm paranoid about the budget.

We have enough. I know we have enough. Wei Hong's bonus came in last month, we maxed out the CPF contributions, the emergency fund has eight months of expenses now. But I keep thinking about what happens if he loses his job, or if one of the kids needs medical treatment not covered by insurance, or if prices keep going up and suddenly eight months becomes four.

My mother used to say "save for nine winters" and I always thought it was superstition. Now I understand. You never know which winter will be the long one.

Nudge: "You said you have enough. Do you believe it?"

Response: Enough is relative. We have enough for now. But things change. I'd rather be overprepared than caught off guard. That's just practical, not paranoid. Can we talk about something else?

Christmas gathering at my sister's place yesterday. All the cousins comparing which schools their kids got into, which scholarships they're aiming for. Jia Hui sat in the corner playing with her cousin's old toys while the adults talked about tuition fees and percentile rankings. I should have pulled her into the conversation. I should have let her be a child.

Wei Hong asked if we really need the third workbook. Said Jia Hui looked tired this week. I told him I'm the one here every day, I see what she needs. He dropped it. Maybe I shouldn't have snapped.

Nudge: "What do you think she needs?"

Response: The same thing every kid needs. To be prepared. To not struggle later. He doesn't see how the other kids are doing, he doesn't sit in those parent WhatsApp groups reading about everyone's tutors and schedules. He gets to be the fun parent who comes home for dinner. I'm the one who has to make sure she's ready for the real world.

New year. Everyone posting their resolutions online. "More quality time with family." "Less screen time." "Be present." I used to make those lists too. Detailed ones, with deadlines and KPIs like I was still writing performance reviews.

This morning Jia Hui asked if we could just play today. No workbooks, no practice. Just play. And I said yes. We built a blanket fort and read picture books inside it while Ming napped. She laughed so hard at the silly voices I made for the characters. For two hours I forgot about enrichment schedules and comprehension scores and whether we're saving enough.

Then tonight, putting her to bed, she said "Mama, today was the best day." And I don't know why but I started crying after I closed her door. Is this what I've been missing? Is this what she needed all along? I keep score so carefully but I'm not sure I'm measuring the right things anymore.

Nudge: "What would measuring "the right things" look like?"

Response: Maybe it's not about measuring at all. Maybe that's the problem. I spent so many years in a job where everything had metrics, targets, quarterly reviews. I brought all of that home with me. Milestone charts. Developmental benchmarks. Comparison tables. But she's not a project. She's a person. A small person who just wants her mother to be there, actually there, not checking boxes in her head. I don't know how to stop keeping score. But today felt like the first time in months I wasn't playing a game where I'm always losing.

Another Sunday close. Jenna asked if she could leave fifteen minutes early because her roommate locked herself out and was sitting on the porch in the rain. I said yes without even thinking about it, then spent the last hour doing her side work plus mine. Not a big deal, really. She looked so stressed when she asked.

Had to explain to the district manager during his surprise visit why we were short-staffed at close. He did that thing where he nods but you can tell he's making a mental note. Whatever. Jenna got home safe. Her roommate has a key now.

Tommy came in today for his shift and he was just—off. Like not making eye contact, moving slow. I pulled him aside after the morning rush, asked if everything was okay, and he told me his grandpa died over the weekend. He'd driven back from Spokane that morning and came straight to work because he didn't want to call in and mess up the schedule.

I sent him home. Told him I'd cover. And honestly it felt right. Like that's exactly what I should've done. He hugged me in the back hallway, which was kind of awkward because we'd never hugged before, but also not awkward at all. Sometimes you just do the thing you'd want someone to do for you.

Nudge: "How'd the rest of the shift go without him?"

Response: Fine. Busy but fine. Not really the point.

Had the conversation with Priya today. The one I've been putting off. She asked me point-blank if she was getting promoted to key holder, and I had to tell her no. Not because she didn't deserve it—she absolutely did—but because corporate froze all promotions until Q1. I could see her face just... close off. She nodded, said she understood, but I know she didn't. How could she?

I wanted to tell her that I'd argued for her. That I'd sent three emails and sat in a forty-five minute call with regional trying to get an exception. But that felt like it would come across as making excuses, or worse, like I wanted credit for trying. So I just sat there and let her think I hadn't fought for it at all.

She took her break right after and didn't say anything to me for the rest of the shift. I keep replaying it. Should I have told her? Would it have changed anything, or just made her feel worse knowing it was out of both our hands? I genuinely don't know. I just know I feel like I let her down, even though I did everything I could. Or did I? Maybe there was another email I could've sent. Another person I could've called.

Nudge: "What stopped you from telling her you fought for it?"

Response: I think I was scared she'd see through me. Like, what if I told her I tried and she could tell I didn't try hard enough? Or what if she asked what exactly I said, and I had to admit I probably could have pushed harder? Easier to just take the hit and let her think I'm the bad guy than to open up and have her confirm it.

District manager pulled me into a call about overtime. Apparently I've approved too many shift swaps. I told him my team has lives outside of work and sometimes things come up. He said that's not his problem.

Jenna brought me a coffee this morning. Just showed up with it before her shift, said it was a thank-you for covering her that Sunday a few weeks back. It was an oat milk latte from the place down the street—the fancy one I'd mentioned once like two months ago. The fact that she remembered felt bigger than the coffee itself.

Weird how a small thing can shift your whole day. I'd been kind of dreading coming in because of all the inventory stuff, but after that I just felt lighter. Like okay, maybe the job isn't just about spreadsheets and schedule conflicts. Sometimes it's just about people noticing each other.

Nudge: "What was it about the inventory stuff that had you dreading it?"

Response: Just boring admin stuff. Takes forever. Doesn't matter now.

Stayed two hours late because Maria's bus didn't come and I wasn't going to make her walk home in the dark. It's not safe. I know it's not in my job description to wait with her, but she's nineteen and her phone was dead and the next bus wasn't for an hour. So I stayed. We sat in the break room and she told me about her mom's chemo appointments and how she's been working doubles to cover the bills.

When I finally got home I just sat in my car in the parking lot for like ten minutes. I'm tired. I want to help everyone but I can't. I physically can't. There's only so many hours and so many shifts I can cover and so many late nights I can stay. But every time I say no, I feel like I'm failing someone. And saying yes is starting to feel like I'm failing myself.

Nudge: "What does failing yourself look like?"

Response: Missing things. My sister's birthday dinner last week, I had to leave early because someone called in. Meals I skip. Sleep I don't get. I keep telling myself it's temporary but I've been saying that for six months.

Priya came up to me today before close and said she appreciated how I run things. That she knows I care about the team. It caught me off guard—we haven't really talked since the promotion conversation. She said she'd been offered a job at the new boba place across the street for more money, but she's staying because of how we treat people here.

I almost cried in the walk-in cooler after she left. I'm so tired. But that—that's why I do it. That's the whole reason. Knowing someone feels seen, feels like they matter. It doesn't fix the overtime problem or the district manager breathing down my neck. But it reminds me there's a point to all of this. There has to be.

Nudge: "What would it mean if there wasn't?"

Response: I don't know. Can't think about that right now.

Locked in the Trane contract for the Edina office park this morning. Six units, maintenance included, eighteen-month term. The margins are thin but it's recurring revenue and that's what matters right now. Sat in the truck after the handshake and just felt the weight come off for a second. Months of quoting and following up and it's done.

Nudge: "What was the weight about?"

Response: Payroll mostly. We added a third tech in February and every month since then I've been watching the numbers tighter than usual. This contract covers his salary through next winter. That's the weight—knowing I can keep the lights on for the guys who depend on me.

Got home from the Edina signing and Karen had dinner going already. Pork chops, which she only makes when she's in a good mood, so that was nice. Told her about the contract and she said congratulations but then asked if I'd thought more about the Comfort Systems recruiter who left a voicemail last week. Regional operations director, salary plus bonus, full benefits. She'd pulled up the Glassdoor page and had it open on the iPad.

I said I'd look at it later. She said I always say that. Which is true. The recruiter calls every eight or nine months and I never call back. Karen thinks it's stubbornness. Maybe it is. But I built this business from one van and a credit card and walking away from it for a salary feels like admitting something I'm not ready to admit.

We didn't argue about it. We never really argue about it. She makes her case, I deflect, we move on to dishes. The pork chops were good. I checked the bank balance on my phone while she loaded the dishwasher, which is a habit I should probably stop doing at the dinner table. Numbers looked fine. They usually do when I check them four times a day.

Had the best day I've had in months and I need to put it somewhere. One of our installs from last year—the dental clinic on France Ave—called because their system was cycling weird. I drove out myself instead of sending Tyler because I remembered the ductwork in that building is unusual, old construction with some modifications from the seventies.

Spent two hours up in the ceiling tracing the problem. Found it. A damper actuator that was wired backwards from the factory, not our install, original equipment. Fixed it with a twenty-dollar part and the office manager looked at me like I'd performed surgery. She said the last company they used would have quoted them a full replacement.

That right there. That's why I do this. Not the money, not the freedom, but the actual work. Knowing the systems, knowing the buildings, solving the puzzle that nobody else caught because nobody else spent two hours in a ceiling when they could've just sold a new unit. I drove home with the windows down even though it was only fifty-five degrees. Karen asked why I was smiling and I said I fixed a damper actuator and she said she had no idea what that meant but she was glad.

Nudge: "What would you lose if you weren't the one up in that ceiling?"

Response: I don't know. Probably nothing that matters to anyone but me. Corporate guys fix things too, just with more paperwork. Anyway it was a good day, that's all.

Sunday and I should be relaxing but I spent the morning going over the books again. Karen's brother Dave called yesterday to say his company is doing a round of layoffs and asked if I had any work for him. Electrical background, good with his hands, reliable. The honest answer is I could use another set of hands for the summer rush. The problem is that hiring Dave means I'm either growing the business or I'm doing my brother-in-law a favor, and those two things need different levels of commitment.

If I bring him on and things slow down in October, I'm the one who has to let Karen's brother go. If I don't bring him on, I'm the guy who could've helped family and chose the spreadsheet instead. Karen didn't ask me directly but she didn't have to. The way she handed me the phone said enough.

Nudge: "Whose business is it when family's involved?"

Long week. Three installs, two service calls, and the compressor supplier shorted us on the Lennox order so I had to drive to the Burnsville warehouse myself. But everything got done. Everyone got paid. Truck's gassed up for tomorrow. I'm sitting on the deck with a beer and the yard looks good. That's enough for tonight.

The Comfort Systems recruiter called again. This time Karen answered and talked to the guy for ten minutes before handing me the phone. She'd already told him about my experience, how long I've been running the business, all of it. Like she was selling me. I took the phone into the garage and told the recruiter I'd think about it, same as always.

Karen said afterward that she's not trying to run my life, she just wants me to have a backup plan. A backup plan. Like what I built is something that needs a safety net instead of being the safety net. I've kept this thing running through a pandemic, through supply chain problems, through a two-month stretch where our biggest client went bankrupt and didn't pay. I don't need a backup plan. I need people to stop acting like doing it yourself is the risky option when it's the only option I can actually control.

Nudge: "Is she wrong about wanting a net, or is it about who's holding it?"

Response: Both, I think. I don't mind having a net. I mind someone else deciding what the net looks like. If I go corporate, the stability is real but it's theirs. They can pull it anytime. What I have now is smaller and harder but it's mine. That's the part she doesn't hear when she talks about benefits and salary bands.

My aunt called again about the civil service exam. She does this every Chuseok season, like clockwork — finds some cousin's daughter who just passed and drops it into conversation as though I asked. I said the same thing I always say. I'm fine. The studio is fine. Rent is covered.

She doesn't believe me. I know because she paused the way she does when she's holding something back, and then she said Umma worries. Which is just her way of saying she worries, but putting it on my mother so it lands harder. I didn't take the bait. Told her I had to go check the kiln.

There was nothing in the kiln. I just sat in the studio and threw a slab that I didn't need. The wheel was cold and the clay was too wet because I hadn't wedged it properly. It didn't matter. I wasn't making anything. I was just moving my hands so I wouldn't think about the conversation. Normal Wednesday.

Gallery owner asked if I'd consider doing a collaboration with a streetwear brand. Custom glaze finishes on sneaker displays. Good money. I said I'd think about it but the whole walk home I kept hearing halmeoni's voice — that clay remembers what your hands were thinking when you shaped it. Felt cheap even considering it.

Woke up to a message from Ji-hye. She got into the residency in Arita. The one we both applied to, the one that would have meant three months studying under a fifth-generation potter and learning noborigama firing. She got it and I didn't, and she told me by text at 7am like it was news about the weather.

I know I should feel happy for her. She's my closest friend in the studio and she works harder than anyone I know. But all I can think about is how I spent two weeks on that application, reworking my portfolio statement, asking my old professor for a letter, calling my mother to say I might be in Japan for the spring so don't plan the family trip yet. All of that for a standard rejection email.

The worst part is I can't even be angry about it openly. Ji-hye keeps texting me details — the studio setup, the housing, the firing schedule — and I keep sending congratulations emojis and exclamation marks. My face hurts from performing enthusiasm on a screen. I closed the chat and went back to bed. It's not even noon.

Nudge: "What were you hoping would be different after Arita?"

Response: That I'd come back with something my family could point to. Studying under a master in Japan — that's something even my aunt would understand. It would have bought me time.

Went to the studio after moping around all morning. Didn't want to, but the pieces for the January show won't fire themselves. Started trimming the set of moon jars I threw last week. The proportions on the third one are actually close to what I've been chasing — that slight asymmetry halmeoni always said separates hand from machine.

Nobody cares about that distinction anymore, probably. The gallery visitors want to know the price and whether it fits on a shelf. But I held the jar up to the window light and the walls were even, the foot was clean, and the curve of the shoulder did exactly what I meant it to do. That matters to me even if it doesn't matter to anyone else.

Nudge: "Who taught you to look at the shoulder curve like that?"

Still at the studio. Eun-bi came by to pick up her tools and asked about the streetwear thing. I told her I'm not doing it and she looked at me like I'd said I don't like money. She said I should be practical, that these collaborations are how young artists build a name now. I told her I already have a name, it's just small.

She means well. But there's something about turning the craft into content that sits wrong with me. Halmeoni spent forty years in the same workshop selling to the same neighborhood. Nobody filmed her. Nobody asked her to put her glazes on handbags. She just made things that people used, and when she died the funeral was standing room only. That's the kind of career that makes sense to me.

End of the day. Checked my account and the last gallery payment was smaller than expected — they took a higher commission this quarter for "marketing costs." Didn't argue. Just transferred Umma's share and looked at what's left. The emergency cushion is thinner than I'd like going into winter.

Nudge: "How thin is thin?"

Response: Two months of rent and studio fees. Maybe two and a half if I skip materials. It's not nothing but it's not enough to stop the math running in my head at 2am.

Sunday morning, studio's empty, just me and the space heater humming. Loading the kiln for the bisque fire — the moon jars plus a few smaller bowls I made for the lunar new year gifts. Every year I make a set for the family, rice bowls with a celadon glaze my grandmother used to mix by feel. I have her ratios written down in a notebook but I still adjust by eye the way she showed me, adding a pinch more calcium when the humidity is high.

Fired up the kiln, set the cone, sat on the floor with tea. The studio smells like dust and wet earth and something burning off the shelves. Nothing urgent. Nothing broken. The jars are in there doing what they'll do and I can't control it from out here. So I'm just sitting. The heater clicks off and on. That's the whole update.

Supervisor meeting today. She said my literature review needs "tightening" before she'll sign off on the methods chapter. What does that even mean? I asked for specifics and got a vague hand gesture toward the citation list. Spent the rest of the afternoon comparing my draft to the review sections of papers that made it into top journals. They're all cleaner somehow, more confident in what they leave out.

Ingrid asked how it went when I got home. I said fine. She's been picking up extra client work to cover the gap our stipend leaves, and I couldn't bring myself to tell her I might be another month behind schedule. She looked tired. I should have said something real.

Nudge: "Why the 'fine'?"

Response: Because admitting I'm struggling feels like admitting I made the wrong choice. We gave up a good apartment, a pension plan, weekends that didn't involve reading deadlines. If I'm not making visible progress, what was all that sacrifice for?

Revised twenty-three pages of the literature review. Supervisor meeting rescheduled to next week because she's at a conference in Berlin. Department coffee hour was cancelled again due to low attendance.

Grant application rejected. The feedback mentioned "insufficient preliminary data" and "unclear pathway to completion." I've been working on this for eighteen months. How much more preliminary can you get?

The younger students in my cohort seem unbothered by these setbacks. They shrug, submit elsewhere, go for beers. Maybe they haven't calculated their monthly burn rate. Maybe they're not watching their wife skip the organic vegetables at the supermarket because the regular ones are cheaper. I keep thinking about my old desk in Stockholm, the ergonomic chair the company provided, the predictable paychecks.

Nudge: "What makes the difference for them?"

Response: They can afford to fail. They're twenty-six, living in student housing, no one depending on them. I'm trying to build something at an age when most people have already built it.

Something small happened today that I keep thinking about. One of my students from the tutorial group, a first-year named Willem, stopped me after class to ask about the regression model I'd explained. He'd been confused during the lecture but didn't want to ask in front of everyone. I walked him through it again on the whiteboard, step by step, and watched his face shift from furrowed to open.

Teaching wasn't supposed to be the point. It's a requirement they attach to our funding, time carved out of research hours. But explaining something clearly enough that another person suddenly gets it, there's a satisfaction in that I hadn't expected. Reminded me of training junior analysts at my old firm, the ones who actually wanted to learn. I was good at that. People would request me for their onboarding.

Walked home the long way along the canal afterward. The light this time of year stays until past nine. For once I wasn't calculating anything. Just watched the boats and felt okay about being here.

Nudge: "What made today different from the grant rejection?"

Response: The grant was about me being judged. This was about someone else learning. I couldn't fail at helping Willem because there was no scoreboard. It was just useful or it wasn't.

Couldn't sleep last night. Ran the numbers again on our savings. If I don't secure additional funding by September, we're looking at difficult choices. Ingrid doesn't know I've started browsing job postings in finance again. Not seriously, just looking. Just in case.

The thought of going back after everything we invested in this feels like a specific kind of defeat. Not just professional, something more personal. Like proof that I can't handle uncertainty, that I need someone else's structure to function. My father worked the same job for thirty-four years. I used to think that was limiting. Now I wonder if he just understood something I didn't.

Nudge: "What did your father understand?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe that wanting more than one thing at once is a recipe for misery.

Department seminar today. One of the second-year students presented preliminary results that were, honestly, sloppy. Half the citations were missing page numbers, the statistical approach was questionable at best. But the professors smiled and nodded and said it was "promising early work."

When I presented six months ago, I got twenty minutes of pointed questions about my methodology. I spent three weeks revising based on their concerns. I'm not saying he didn't deserve encouragement, but the standards seem flexible in ways I don't understand. Maybe they see him as someone with time to improve. Maybe they see me as someone who should know better by now.

Same day, later. Ingrid found the job postings in my browser history. I expected anger but she just sat down on the bed and asked if I wanted to talk. Told her everything. The grant rejection, the supervisor feedback, the runway calculations, the feeling that I'm competing against people who have nothing to lose.

She listened and then said something I hadn't considered. She said we came here so I could find out if I could do this, and that finding out includes the possibility that I can't, and that wouldn't make the choice wrong. It would just make it complete. I don't know if I agree with her logic, but hearing someone say it out loud made the weight feel different. Lighter, somehow. Like the worst case scenario has been acknowledged and we're still sitting here, still together, still figuring it out.

Arjun's old company reached out to him about a relocation package to Bangalore. Good salary, housing allowance, school fees covered for Meera. He brought it up over dinner like it was just information, but I could tell he was testing the water. If we moved, I'd have to give up my lab placement and start over somewhere, if I even could.

The sensible thing would have been to say let's talk about it seriously. He's been carrying us financially for four years now and the offer was generous. Instead I just said I'm not ready to leave yet. He didn't push it. But I spent the rest of the night doing the math in my head — how much longer the PhD will take, how much we've burned through savings, whether Meera even remembers what it was like when we weren't watching every expense. I keep choosing this and I don't fully know why anymore.

Nudge: "What would finishing feel like, if not relief?"

Response: Like proof that it was worth it. That I didn't just burn four years and all our savings on something selfish. But sometimes I wonder if I'm just trying to outlast the doubt at this point, like if I keep going long enough the answer will show up on its own.

Submitted the revised chapter draft to my supervisor this morning. Picked Meera up from after-school care, stopped at the wet market for vegetables, made dal and rice for dinner. Nothing remarkable. Normal day.

Meera drew a picture of me at my desk with all these little papers flying around and wrote "Amma working hard" underneath. I stuck it on the wall next to my monitor. Some days that's enough to keep going.

Got an email today from a former colleague, Deepa, about a senior position opening at the water authority back in Chennai. Permanent role, government pension, the whole thing. She said she thought of me immediately. My mother would cry with happiness if I took it. Appa would finally stop asking pointed questions about "what the plan is." Arjun's parents would stop whispering about how their son married a woman who can't settle down.

I sat with the email open for maybe twenty minutes. I could picture the whole life — the flat in Adyar, Meera going to the same school I went to, weekend lunches at my parents' house. Predictable. Comfortable. Everything mapped out for the next thirty years. And I felt this thing in my chest, not panic exactly but something close. Like the walls of that life were already closing in even though I haven't said yes to anything. I know the stipend is barely enough. I know Arjun is tired of being the only one earning properly. I know my savings are basically gone. I know all of this.

But I closed the email and went back to my data. I'll probably tell Deepa thanks but not now, maybe later, which is what I always say. The part I can't explain to anyone is that the uncertainty here feels more honest than the certainty there. That sounds ridiculous when I say it out loud but there it is.

Nudge: "What does 'honest' mean here — honest about what?"

Response: About what I actually want, I think. The Chennai job would be fine. Good, even. But it would be someone else's version of my life. The one everyone expected me to have. Here I'm struggling but at least the struggle is mine.
Lab equipment broke down again. Spent the whole day troubleshooting instead of running samples. Came home to Meera already asleep and leftover rice in the cooker.

Amma called tonight and casually mentioned that Deepa's husband just bought a new flat in OMR. Then she said, "You know Deepa was asking about you, whether you've thought about that position." So apparently the whole family knows now. Great.

I told her I'm focused on my research and she did that thing where she goes quiet for a few seconds and then says "of course, of course, we just worry." Arjun was listening from the other room and afterward he said maybe we should at least talk about a timeline. He wasn't being pushy about it but the fact that he's now aligned with my mother is — I don't know. I told him my timeline is when I finish and he said that's not a timeline, that's a hope. He's not wrong. But the alternative is giving up something I've worked four years on because everyone else is tired of waiting. I'm tired too. But tired isn't the same as done.

Nudge: "What would it take for tired to become done?"

Had a good meeting with my supervisor today. She said the fieldwork chapter is strong and that the analysis is heading in the right direction. First time in weeks I've felt like I actually know what I'm doing. She also mentioned a conference in March where I could present, which would be good for the CV.

Afterward I walked to the hawker centre near campus and got mee goreng and just sat there eating by myself for a while. It was raining and the whole place smelled like wet concrete and frying oil. I watched the aunties running the stalls — same thing every day, same dishes, same rhythm — and I thought about how there's a kind of steadiness in doing work you've chosen, even if nobody else sees why you chose it.

I called Arjun on the way home and told him about the conference. He sounded genuinely happy for me, which I needed more than I realized. I know this path doesn't make sense on paper. The money is bad, the timeline is uncertain, and every aunty and uncle back home thinks I've lost the plot. But today felt like one of those days where I remembered why I started. That has to count for something.

Spent most of the afternoon sourcing fabric swatches with Diya at this tiny warehouse in Camp. The guy running the place kept talking about minimum order quantities and I could see her doing math in her head, getting stressed. I just wanted to touch everything, feel the weight of it. Found this insane indigo block-print that would be perfect for the bomber jackets we've been sketching.

On the rickshaw back I was thinking about how three months ago I was sitting in a lecture hall pretending to care about supply chain management and now I'm actually doing it, badly, in real life. The irony isn't lost on me. Mum called while I was out but I let it ring. Didn't feel like hearing about Priya's wedding venue shortlist again.

Nudge: "What happens when you do pick up those calls?"

Response: Same script every time. How's the business, when am I coming home, did I hear Priya's news. I just run out of ways to sound enthusiastic.

Pop-up at Koregaon Park went well. Sold eleven pieces including two of the bomber prototypes. One girl asked if we had an Instagram — we do, but it's barely set up. Diya handled the money stuff while I talked to people. That part I'm good at. Strangers telling me they like something I made with my hands, that's a specific kind of high.

Afterwards we went to this new rooftop bar Adi told me about. Spent way too much on cocktails but the music was perfect and the city looked ridiculous from up there. Diya said it was our "revenue celebration." We made maybe eight thousand rupees today and spent half of it on drinks and that felt exactly right.

Meeting with a guy who runs a boutique in Viman Nagar. He wants to stock five designs on consignment. The terms aren't great — he takes forty percent — but Diya says the visibility is worth it. I kept looking around his shop thinking about whether our stuff would even fit next to all that boho tourist stuff.

Adi came over after. We ordered biryani and watched some terrible thriller on Netflix. He mentioned his lease is up in April and sort of hinted at us getting a place together. I changed the subject to the boutique deal. Not sure why. It's not like I haven't thought about it.

Nudge: "You've thought about it but changed the subject — what came up for you in that moment?"

Response: Felt like if I said yes it would become this whole plan, this whole thing with routines and splitting rent and groceries. I like how we are now where it's easy.

Late now, Adi left an hour ago. Finished stitching the last sample for the consignment order and my fingers are sore but the jacket looks exactly how I pictured it. Small win. Going to sleep actually satisfied for once.

Went home for lunch yesterday because Dad's birthday. Mum cornered me in the kitchen to ask if I've "thought about what comes next" if the brand doesn't work out. Priya was right there smiling with her ring and her Infosys lanyard still around her neck like some kind of prop. I know Mum means well but I'm so tired of being the cautionary tale at family gatherings.

I told her I don't have a backup plan because this is the plan. She got quiet in that way she does. Dad just ate his cake and said the jacket I brought him was nice, which honestly was enough. I don't need everyone to get it. The brand is growing, I'm paying my share of rent, I'm not asking anyone for money. I don't know what else there is to prove.

Nudge: "You said Dad's comment was enough — was it actually?"

Can't sleep, brain won't stop. Thinking about the consignment thing and whether it's a step up or just giving our margins away for the ego of seeing our label in a shop. Diya ran the numbers and we'd need to sell through at least seventy percent to break even after materials. That's tight. But then again if we don't get the stuff in front of new people it just sits in our flat.

Also still chewing on the Adi thing from the other night. It's not that I don't want to be with him, it's that the second you sign a lease with someone the whole shape of your days changes. You eat dinner at the same time, you watch the same shows, you wake up and there's no surprise about who's next to you. That sounds awful when I write it out but I don't mean it in a cruel way. I just like having space where I can decide at midnight to rework a pattern or go meet Diya for chai without explaining.

Maybe I'm overthinking this. He didn't bring it up again and maybe he won't. Part of me kind of wants him to, just so I know he actually means it instead of it being some lease-expiry convenience thing. I should probably eat something, I skipped dinner and my stomach is doing that acidic thing.

Diya wants to pause the brand for a month because she got offered a paid internship at some design studio in Bangalore. She says it's temporary and she'll be back but it felt like the floor shifted. Sat on the balcony after she told me just staring at traffic, not sure what I'm supposed to do with half a brand.

Nudge: "What part of this scares you most — the business or doing it alone?"

Amma called this morning while I was waiting for an order at the hawker center and she was crying before she even said hello. Priya's college fees went up again, some new lab component they're adding to the program. I told her not to worry, that I would figure it out, but my hands were shaking a bit when I hung up. The order came through right after and I had to ride with that sitting in my chest.

I ran the numbers on the way home. If I pick up the late night slots Monday through Thursday on top of the regular daytime rides, I can probably cover the gap. That's four extra hours a day though. My back has been acting up from all the riding already. Kumar keeps telling me to see the doctor at the polyclinic but when exactly am I supposed to go. Every hour I sit in a waiting room is money not coming in.

I keep thinking about what Appa would have done. He worked the rubber plantation six days a week and never complained, at least not where we could hear. But I also remember how tired he always looked. I don't want to end up like that but I also can't imagine calling Amma back and saying actually we need to find another way for Priya's fees.

Something good happened today. Mrs. Lim from the fourth floor introduced me to her son-in-law who runs a small renovation company. He said he's been looking for someone reliable to do basic plumbing and electrical work for his residential jobs, not full-time but steady weekend work. The pay is better per hour than delivery riding.

I told him about the things I've been fixing around the block and he asked if I could come by a site in Bedok on Saturday to show him what I can do. It feels like the first real opening in months. If it works out, I could drop some of the late night delivery slots and still cover Priya's fees without grinding myself into the ground.

Called Amma to tell her and she immediately started worrying about whether the renovation work is safe, whether I'll be climbing ladders, whether the boss is trustworthy. That's how she shows she cares, I know that. I just wished for one moment she could say that sounds great, Rajesh, I'm glad. But I could hear the relief underneath the worrying, so I'll take it.

Nudge: "What would it mean to you if she had said that?"

Response: It would mean she sees me, not just what I provide. Amma has always been practical, it's how she survived after Appa died. Everything gets filtered through is it safe, is it sustainable, will it last. I understand why. But sometimes I wish someone in my family would just be proud of me for figuring things out here on my own. Priya does, actually. She sent me a voice note last week saying she couldn't believe I fixed the neighbor's water heater. Small thing but I replayed it twice.

Good day on the road. Hit my target by 4pm which almost never happens. Used the extra time to pick up some curry puff for the uncles who play chess downstairs, they always wave at me when I ride past and I figured why not. Uncle Rajan tried to teach me a Sicilian opening again. I'm terrible at chess but he laughs every time I make a bad move and honestly that's worth more than winning.

Nudge: "What draws you to spending your free time with them?"

Priya called at midnight her time which is never a good sign. One of her classmates had some kind of breakdown during clinicals and Priya was the one who stayed with her at the hospital for three hours while the girl's family was contacted. She sounded rattled. I stayed on the phone for forty minutes just letting her talk, even though I had an early morning slot booked.

Now I can't sleep. Partly because I'm worried about Priya and partly because I keep thinking about what happens if she burns out before she even finishes the degree. She's like me, she can't watch someone struggle and just walk past. I don't know if that's a strength or a problem. Probably both. I should have told her to look after herself first but I didn't because honestly I don't follow that advice either so who am I to give it.

Saturday site visit went better than I expected. Mr. Tan, Mrs. Lim's son-in-law, watched me fix a leaking pipe joint and re-wire a light switch and said he'd give me two days a week starting next month. Shook my hand properly, looked me in the eye, said "you clearly know what you're doing." I haven't heard that from a boss figure in a long time.

Came back and told my flatmate Kumar and he said we should celebrate so we went to the roti prata place near the MRT. I kept doing the math in my head over dinner. Two days renovation plus four days delivery is still a lot but the hourly rate is almost double, so I could actually rest on Sundays for once. Might even have time to call home when I'm not half asleep.

Nudge: "You mention resting on Sundays as something you 'could' do. What usually wins when you have a free day?"

Long week. Did the late night slots three days in a row because one of the other riders, Suresh, had to fly back to Chennai for a family emergency and I told the coordinator I could cover. Stupid probably but Suresh helped me when I first started and didn't know the routes. You don't forget that. My knee is swollen and I've been icing it with a bag of frozen peas that Kumar keeps threatening to eat.

Mrs. Lim knocked on the door today with a container of herbal soup. She said I looked thin the last time she saw me and that I remind her of her late husband who also never knew when to stop working. I didn't know what to say to that so I just thanked her and ate the whole thing standing in the kitchen. It was really good. I can't remember the last time someone cooked for me who wasn't being paid for it.

Priya sent money. Not much, maybe fifty dollars equivalent, from her part-time tutoring. She said it was for me to see the doctor about my back. I almost sent it right back but something stopped me. Maybe the soup. Maybe being tired enough that my stubbornness ran out of fuel. I booked the polyclinic appointment for Monday. We'll see if I actually go.

Nudge: "What made you keep Priya's money instead of sending it back?"

Response: Because she would have been hurt. She's working that tutoring job on top of her studies specifically to send me something, and sending it back would be like saying her effort doesn't count. I know that feeling. When I first started sending money home Amma tried to refuse and it made me feel small, like I couldn't contribute. So I kept Priya's fifty dollars because I remembered what it felt like to have someone reject what you're trying to give them. The back appointment is real though. I need to actually go.

Sat on the balcony after dinner with a glass of something cold and the city was doing that thing where the light turns everything golden. I could hear Camille laughing on the phone inside. I nearly didn't come home after school because some of the other trainees were going for drinks, but I wanted this more. Sounds stupid maybe but I picked the right evening. The air smelled like bread from downstairs and my legs were tired in the good way, like I'd actually done something useful today. The kids were chaos as usual but we got through the maths block without anyone crying, which I'm counting as a win. Sometimes the small stuff is enough.

Nudge: "What made you sure about coming home?"

Response: Honestly I just knew I'd be sitting in a loud bar pretending to enjoy myself when all I really wanted was quiet. I like those people fine but I already spent all day performing. The balcony doesn't ask anything from me.

My mentor observed my lesson on Friday and said I need to be "firmer with boundaries." She means discipline. I know she's right, probably. But I let the kids talk during transitions because they're six and they just want to tell each other stuff. She wrote it up as an area for improvement.

The thing that's sitting wrong is I smiled and nodded and said I'd work on it. I didn't push back at all. Didn't even say what I actually think, which is that making them sit in silence for thirty seconds between activities is pointless. I just folded. Walked out of the meeting feeling like I'd agreed to be someone I'm not and now I have to actually do it on Monday.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying what you think?"

Response: She's the one signing off on my certification. That's the honest answer. I don't want to be difficult. Everyone else in the programme just does what they're told and it works out fine for them. I don't know why I can't just do the same without it gnawing at me afterwards.

Tried the firmer boundaries thing today and it felt horrible. I made them wait in silence before moving to reading and one of the little ones looked up at me like I'd taken something away from him. Which I had. I kept a straight face and got through it and my mentor wasn't even watching.

I don't know who I did it for. Camille asked how my day was and I said fine. Didn't feel like explaining the whole thing because she'd just say I'm overthinking it, which is probably true but doesn't help. Ate leftover pasta standing up in the kitchen and went to bed at nine. Can't tell if I'm tired or just flat.

My mum called tonight and asked how the placement was going. I told her it was going well. She asked if I was eating properly and sleeping enough and I said yes to both even though the second one is debatable. She worries, and I don't want to give her anything to worry about, so I edit.

It's not lying exactly. It's just that if I say "actually I'm struggling with this one aspect of teaching" she'll spiral into whether I've chosen the right career, whether I should come home for a bit, whether Lyon is too far. I've watched her do it with my brother when he mentioned one bad week at his job and suddenly she was looking up flats near their house for him. I can't open that door.

So the call was fine. Pleasant. We talked about her garden and my aunt's birthday and whether I'd be back for it in September. I said probably, which is true. She sounded happy. I sounded happy. Everyone performed their part.

I don't think there's anything wrong with that. People manage their parents. It's normal. I just wish it didn't take so much energy, the constant calibrating of what to share and what to keep back. By the time I hung up I was more tired than before the call started. Made myself a cup of tea and watched something mindless. Fine evening, really. Nothing to complain about.

Nudge: "Who are you performing for besides your mum?"

Response: That's a bit much. I just meant we keep things light, it's how we've always been. Anyway the point is the call went fine, not everything needs to be a big revelation.

Absolutely shattered. End-of-module assessments for the kids this week and I've been prepping materials every evening. My eyes hurt from the screen. But there was this moment today during the reading assessment where Nour, who's been struggling all term, read a full page out loud without stopping. She looked at me after and I just gave her a thumbs up and she grinned so wide.

That's it. That's the whole thing I'm holding onto tonight. The rest of the day was paperwork and a tense staff meeting about next year's timetable where I sat there saying nothing because the permanent teachers were arguing and I'm still a trainee so I just kept quiet. Which is fine, that's my place right now. I don't have standing to weigh in on scheduling decisions.

But Nour's face. She's been so anxious about reading and she did it. I didn't even do anything special, just followed the programme and gave her time. Sometimes you don't need to reinvent things.

Camille brought home macarons from that place near her office and we ate them on the balcony even though it was too hot. The pistachio ones are unreasonably good. I told her about Nour and she said "see, you're good at this" and I let myself believe it for a minute. Going to sleep well tonight. Body's wrecked but my head is okay.
