Nudge: "What would you have said at that meeting if you could?"

Response: Probably that the Wednesday afternoon slot they're fighting over doesn't matter as much as they think it does. But I'd never actually say that. I barely know the politics and I don't want to step on anyone. It's smarter to just listen for now and figure out how things work before opening my mouth.

Friday night and I'm sitting here instead of going to the party at Thomas's place. Everyone from the programme is going. Camille went already, she looked great, asked me three times if I was sure. I said I was tired which is true but also I just didn't want to go and I couldn't say that because then it becomes a whole conversation about why not and am I okay and should she stay.

The real reason is last time at Thomas's I had too much wine and said something about our supervisor that wasn't mean exactly but was honest and I could feel the room shift. Mathilde changed the subject really fast. Nobody brought it up again but I've been thinking about it for weeks. The comment wasn't even bad, I just said she gives vague feedback, which she does, but saying it out loud in front of people felt like I'd broken some rule I didn't know existed.

So now I'm on the sofa with leftover macarons and a show about renovating houses in Normandy and I feel weird about it. Not the staying in part, I genuinely like this. But the fact that I'm staying in because of something I said three weeks ago that probably nobody remembers. That's the part that's sitting wrong. I keep replaying it and adjusting — what I should have said, how I should have laughed it off, whether I should have just kept my mouth shut like I usually do. The mouth-shut version of me has fewer problems but she also has fewer macarons and is standing in Thomas's kitchen right now pretending to enjoy bad speakers and warm beer.

Got my mid-placement review today. "Meets expectations." Not exceeds, just meets. My mentor said I'm reliable, well-prepared, good with the children, but I need to contribute more in team settings and be more assertive in classroom management. The same things again.

I wanted to say: I am good with the children precisely because I'm not assertive in the way you mean. I let them breathe. But I didn't say that. I thanked her for the feedback and said I'd keep working on it. She seemed satisfied. The review goes in my file and that's that.

Afterwards Thomas asked a few of us how it went and everyone said fine. Mathilde got "exceeds" in classroom management and was obviously pleased. I said mine went well too, which it did, technically. Meets expectations is fine. It's literally the standard. I don't know why I'm annoyed about it.

Actually I do. I'm annoyed because I think I'm better than meets expectations but the things I'm good at aren't the things they measure. They don't have a box for "made a scared kid feel safe enough to read out loud." They have a box for "implements behaviour management strategies consistently." I do implement them. I just don't enjoy it and apparently that shows.

Had dinner with Camille and we split a bottle of wine and she told me about some drama at her office and it was exactly what I needed. Not to think about my own stuff for an hour. Good food, good company, clean kitchen afterwards. The week is done.

Nudge: "Is 'meets expectations' actually fine with you?"

Response: No. But what am I going to do, argue with the rubric? I know what they want and I know I could give it to them if I tried harder. I just don't want to become that teacher. The one the kids go quiet around. I'd rather meet expectations and keep the classroom feeling like a place they actually want to be.

Completed the Q1 review presentation today. Numbers came in 12% above target for my division. Will send to regional director tomorrow morning.

Nudge: "What does hitting that 12% mean to you?"

Response: Honestly? It means I'm still relevant. At 57, there's always this question in the back of my mind—can the younger managers do what I do? Seeing those numbers tells me I still bring something they can't match yet. Experience has value.

Found out Carlos from Guadalajara got invited to the leadership summit and I didn't. Thirty-four years with this company and they invite someone with eight. I don't even know what to say.

Nudge: "What would that invitation have proven?"

Response: It's not about proving anything. It's about respect for a track record. But maybe I'm just tired today. There's a lot of context the higher-ups might have that I don't know about.

Spent some time this weekend thinking about the Carlos situation. I realize I was more upset than the situation warranted. He's doing good work in Guadalajara, and the summit is about digital transformation—an area he's genuinely stronger in than I am.

What bothered me wasn't Carlos. It was feeling like my contributions are becoming invisible. I've hit every target for six quarters straight, but somewhere along the way, targets stopped feeling like enough. The goalposts moved and nobody told me.

Had my monthly one-on-one with my director. I asked directly about the VP track and where I stand. She said my operational numbers are "exemplary" but that they're looking for leaders who can "build capability in others." She mentioned mentorship programs.

I've trained dozens of supervisors over the years. Most of them are running plants now. But apparently that doesn't count unless it's in some formal program with tracking metrics. Now I need to figure out how to make what I already do more visible, or start doing something new at 57.

Nudge: "How does it feel to quantify mentorship?"
Lucia told me I've been distant at home. She's right. I keep thinking about work even at dinner. Need to find some balance but also don't want to lose momentum on the VP push.

Today was difficult. The quarterly town hall happened and they announced promotions across the region. Three VPs named—none of them me. One of them is someone I trained back in 2018. Daniel, the kid who used to ask me basic questions about inventory systems. Now he's VP of Logistics for Central America.

I should be happy for him. Part of me is. But mostly I feel like I'm watching my own career highlights reel being played by someone else. All those late nights, all those targets exceeded, all those crises managed—what was it for? I built a machine that runs so smoothly they forgot someone built it.

Lucia saw my face when I came home. She didn't say anything, just made me coffee and sat with me. I couldn't talk about it. What would I say? That I'm jealous of my own mentee? That feels shameful to even write.

I keep thinking about my leather notebook. Every promotion, every bonus, every recognition. When did the entries stop mattering? Or maybe they always mattered less than I thought. Maybe what mattered was that I thought I was building toward something, and today I realized I might have already arrived—and this is it.

Nudge: "What would 'enough' look like now?"

Woke up early today, couldn't sleep. Started thinking about what my director said about building capability in others. Then about Daniel. He's calling me next week to "pick my brain" about some challenges in his new role. He didn't have to do that.

Maybe that's the thing. I keep score one way—titles, metrics, recognition from above. But there's another scoreboard I've been ignoring. The people who learned something from me and kept climbing. The plants that run well because someone I trained is running them. That doesn't get me VP. But maybe VP isn't the only way to measure thirty-four years. I don't know. Still thinking.

Had a small win today. Closed a bulk deal with a buyer in Shenzhen, margins were solid, the whole thing came together in like two hours from first DM to payment confirmed. These are the moments where I feel like I actually know what I'm doing. Not pretending, not faking it. Just reading the situation right and moving.

My mum asked about it at dinner and I gave her the short version. She nodded but didn't say much. A year ago that would've stung. Tonight it just felt fine. I don't need her to get it. The deal speaks for itself and I know what it took.

Nudge: "What's different about tonight?"

Spent the afternoon sourcing new drops, scrolling through pages of the same stuff everyone else is flipping. Getting harder to find edges. Might need to branch into vintage or accessories, something less saturated. Called James about it and he's keen to explore Taipei suppliers. Brain's moving fast today, not in a bad way, just restless.

Nudge: "Bored or scared of stalling?"

Response: Neither really. Just how my brain works, always scanning for the next angle. Anyway I need to finalize the Taipei logistics before I spiral into planning mode.

Auntie May came for dinner. The usual questions: when am I going back to school, do I have a real plan, what happens when this "internet thing" stops working. I smiled and gave polite non-answers. Changed the subject to her son's new job because I knew that would keep her busy for twenty minutes.

It's a skill at this point, steering conversations away from anything that makes them uncomfortable about me. I don't even get annoyed anymore. It's just the cost of sitting at that table without ruining everyone's evening. My cousin Jason does the same thing with his music career. We exchanged a look across the table. That was enough.

Dad asked me this morning if I'd consider taking a part-time role at Uncle Raymond's logistics company. Stable income, benefits, "good for your resume." I said I'd think about it. He looked relieved, which is probably the part that's bothering me most.

The business is actually growing. March numbers are tracking 40% above February. I have a potential partnership with a streetwear brand in Seoul that could change the whole trajectory. But none of that registers with him because it doesn't look like a job. I didn't bring up the Seoul thing. No point until there's a signed contract he can hold in his hands.

Nudge: "Are you thinking about it or just buying time?"

Been sitting with the uncle's company thing all day. Part of me knows the smart move is to say no and keep building. The numbers back me up. But there's this other part, the part that watched my dad's face relax when I said I'd consider it, and that part wants to just take the damn job so everyone can stop worrying.

It's not even about the money or the role. It's about what saying no does to the room. Every holiday, every family dinner, I'm the loose thread. The one who doesn't fit the pattern. And I can handle that most days but sometimes I wonder if I'm burning through goodwill I'm going to need later. Like what happens when I actually need their support for something and they've already written me off as the kid who wouldn't listen.

The Seoul partnership could be real. If it lands, it validates everything. But if it doesn't, I'm just the dropout who turned down a stable offer to chase another long shot. I keep toggling between these two versions of how this plays out and neither one lets me sleep.

Nudge: "What support are you actually afraid of losing?"

Response: Honestly, probably not financial. They'd help if things went really bad regardless. It's more like... being taken seriously. Right now I'm tolerated. If I keep going against what they want and it doesn't pay off spectacularly, I become the cautionary tale they tell my younger cousins. That's the version I can't stomach.

Mum just came into my room to say dad's feelings were hurt that I haven't given an answer about uncle's company yet. It's been twelve hours. Twelve. I told her I was still thinking and she gave me the look, the one that means she's already decided what the right answer is and is waiting for me to arrive at it.

I'm not being ungrateful. I know they're looking out for me. But every time they frame some fallback option as "just in case," what they're really saying is they don't think this works. And maybe they're right, but I need to figure that out on my own terms, not because I got guilted into hedging. Anyway. I told her I'd talk to dad tomorrow. That bought me another day at least.

Got rejected from another group show today. Third one this month. I know my work is good, the feedback from the last curator even said my pieces were "visually compelling" but "lacked cohesion with the exhibition theme." I keep telling myself that I need to find the right fit, but sitting here surrounded by half-finished canvases and a pile of rejection emails, it's hard not to question everything.

The worst part is I was so bored with my last series that I started experimenting with metalwork halfway through. Now my portfolio looks like three different artists made it. Maybe that's the problem. Everyone keeps saying to develop a "signature style" but the thought of making the same kind of work for years makes me want to throw my brushes in the trash.

Nudge: "What would it mean for you to have a 'signature style'?"

Response: Honestly? It feels like a cage. Like agreeing to draw the same box over and over because galleries can sell it easier. But maybe I'm also scared that if I pick one thing, I'll find out I'm not actually great at it. Easier to be "promising" in five directions than mediocre in one.

Still thinking about yesterday. Couldn't sleep so I went back through my old sketchbooks from freshman year. Forgot how much I used to draw just for myself back then, weird little creatures and impossible architecture, no thought about whether it would sell or fit a gallery theme. Somewhere around junior year I started thinking about everything in terms of what it could become, what door it might open.

Worked my gallery shift today. It was dead, which meant I had hours to just sit there and stare at the current exhibition. These stark minimalist paintings that all look almost identical to me, just slight variations in shade and proportion. The artist is represented in galleries across the country. People pay five figures for those canvases. And I genuinely don't know if I'm jealous or horrified.

The owner came in toward closing and we got to talking. She said something that stuck with me: "The artists who last aren't the ones who burn brightest, they're the ones who find something they can sustain." I nodded like I understood but I'm not sure I do. What if I'm just not built for sustainability? What if I need the burn to keep going?

Applied to a residency in New Mexico. Three months in the desert with studio space and a stipend. Deadline was today. Spent all morning putting together the application materials. Portfolio, artist statement, project proposal.

For the proposal I wrote about wanting to explore the relationship between geological time and human impermanence using found desert materials. It sounds pretentious when I read it back but that's what they want to hear. The real reason I want to go is simpler: I'm bored here. Same coffee shop, same studio, same loop of galleries on First Thursday. I think I work better when everything around me is unfamiliar.

Started tallying up my applications this year. Fourteen residencies, eight group shows, three grants. Got into two group shows and one residency that I ended up turning down because it conflicted with a commission. That's a fifteen percent success rate if you're generous about the math. Industry standard is apparently around ten percent so I guess I'm doing okay. But okay isn't what I'm aiming for.

Nudge: "You turned down a residency for a commission?"

Commission is done but I hate it. The client wanted "bold and colorful" and I gave them exactly that and now it's sitting in their corporate lobby looking like a very expensive screensaver. Got paid though.

Nudge: "What made you take this commission in the first place?"

Spent ten hours in the studio today working on a new piece. Lost track of time completely. My back hurts and I forgot to eat lunch but there's something on the canvas now that wasn't there this morning and that's enough.

Got the email this afternoon. I'm in. New Mexico residency, three months starting in March. I read the acceptance twice and then I just sat there staring at my screen for a while. Not jumping up and down, not immediately texting everyone I know. Just this weird quiet feeling, like something clicking into place.

Called my mom to tell her. She's worried about me being alone in the desert, which is funny because I'm alone in my studio most of the time anyway. But she's excited too. She asked if I was going to use this to "focus on one style finally" and I managed not to sigh into the phone. I told her I'm going to use it to make the best work I've ever made, whatever form that takes.

Started planning what to bring. Limited luggage means I have to choose which materials matter most. I'm thinking oil paints, my good brushes, sketchbooks, and nothing else. Let the desert fill in the gaps. Maybe that's what I've been missing, not a signature style but just enough constraint to push against. Every time I've made something I was proud of, it was because I had a problem to solve, not unlimited options. Something to think about.

My success rate for applications this year ended at nineteen percent. Still not where I want to be. But for once the math feels less important than what's actually happening. I'm going to make art in the desert. That's real.

Nudge: "You said constraint helps you make better work. How does that connect to how you feel about having a signature style?"

Response: Maybe they're not as different as I thought. A signature style doesn't have to mean repetition, it could be a set of constraints I choose for myself rather than ones galleries or clients impose. The desert residency is forcing me to pick only essential materials, and I'm actually looking forward to that limitation. If I could do the same thing with my artistic approach, choose my own boundaries instead of resisting all boundaries, maybe that's the middle ground. Still figuring it out.
